text.Rotherham: And, when the centurion heard [that], he went unto the captain, and reported, saying—What art thou going to do? For, this man, is, a Roman?
text.KJV: When the centurion heard that, he went and told the chief captain, saying, Take heed what thou doest: for this man is a Roman.
text.NABRE: When the centurion heard this, he went to the cohort commander and reported it, saying, “What are you going to do? This man is a Roman citizen.”
text.DRB: Which the Centurion hearing, went to the Tribune, and told him, saying: What wilt thou do? for this man is a citizen of Rome.

text.NRSV: The tribune came and asked Paul, “Tell me, are you a Roman citizen?” And he said, “Yes.”
text.Rotherham: And the captain, coming up, said to him—Tell me! Art, thou, a, Roman? And, he, said—Yea!
text.KJV: Then the chief captain came, and said unto him, Tell me, art thou a Roman? He said, Yea.
text.NABRE: Then the commander came and said to him, “Tell me, are you a Roman citizen?” “Yes,” he answered.
text.DRB: And the Tribune coming, said to him: Tell me, art thou a Roman? But he said: Yea.

text.NRSV: The tribune answered, “It cost me a large sum of money to get my citizenship.” Paul said, “But I was born a citizen.”
text.Rotherham: And the captain answered—I, for a large sum, this citizenship acquired! And, Paul, said—But, I, am even, [free-] born!
text.KJV: And the chief captain answered, With a great sum obtained I this freedom. And Paul said, But I was free born.
text.NABRE: The commander replied, “I acquired this citizenship for a large sum of money.” Paul said, “But I was born one.”
text.DRB: And the Tribune answered: I obtained this city with a great sum. And Paul said: But I was also born to it.

text.NRSV: Immediately those who were about to examine him drew back from him; and the tribune also was afraid, for he realized that Paul was a Roman citizen and that he had bound him.
text.Rotherham: Straightway, therefore, they who were about to put him to the test, withdrew from him; and, even the captain, was struck with fear, when he found out he was, a Roman, and because, him, he had bound.
text.KJV: Then straightway they departed from him which should have examined him: and the chief captain also was afraid, after he knew that he was a Roman, and because he had bound him.
text.NABRE: At once those who were going to interrogate him backed away from him, and the commander became alarmed when he realized that he was a Roman citizen and that he had had him bound.
text.DRB: Immediately therefore they departed from him that were to torment him. The Tribune also feared after he understood that he was a citizen of Rome, and because he had bound him.

text.NRSV: Since he wanted to find out what Paul was being accused of by the Jews, the next day he released him and ordered the chief priests and the entire council to meet. He brought Paul down and had him stand before them.
text.Rotherham: But, on the morrow, being minded to get to know the certainty as to why he was being accused by the Jews, he released him, and ordered the High-priests and all the High-council to come together; and, bringing down Paul, set him before them.
text.KJV: On the morrow, because he would have known the certainty wherefore he was accused of the Jews, he loosed him from his bands, and commanded the chief priests and all their council to appear, and brought Paul down, and set him before them.
text.NABRE: Paul Before the Sanhedrin. The next day, wishing to determine the truth about why he was being accused by the Jews, he freed him and ordered the chief priests and the whole Sanhedrin to convene. Then he brought Paul down and made him stand before them.
text.DRB: But the next day meaning to know more diligently for what cause he was accused of the Jews, he loosed him, and commanded the Priests to come together and all the Council: & bringing forth Paul, he set him among them.

text.KJV: And Paul, earnestly beholding the council, said, Men and brethren, I have lived in all good conscience before God until this day.
text.NABRE: Paul looked intently at the Sanhedrin and said, “My brothers, I have conducted myself with a perfectly clear conscience before God to this day.”
text.NRSV: While Paul was looking intently at the council he said, “Brothers, up to this day I have lived my life with a clear conscience before God.”
text.DRB: And Paul looking upon the Council, said: Men brethren, I with all good conscience have conversed before God, until this present day.
text.Rotherham: And Paul, looking steadfastly, at the High-council, said—Brethren! I, in all good conscience have used my citizenship for God until this day.

text.KJV: And the high priest Ananias commanded them that stood by him to smite him on the mouth.
text.NABRE: The high priest Ananias ordered his attendants to strike his mouth.
text.NRSV: Then the high priest Ananias ordered those standing near him to strike him on the mouth.
text.DRB: And the high Priest Ananias commanded them that stood by him, to smite him on the mouth.
text.Rotherham: And, the High-priest, Ananias, ordered them that stood by him, to be smiting him on the mouth.

text.KJV: Then said Paul unto him, God shall smite thee, thou whited wall: for sittest thou to judge me after the law, and commandest me to be smitten contrary to the law?
text.NABRE: Then Paul said to him, “God will strike you, you whitewashed wall. Do you indeed sit in judgment upon me according to the law and yet in violation of the law order me to be struck?”
text.NRSV: At this Paul said to him, “God will strike you, you whitewashed wall! Are you sitting there to judge me according to the law, and yet in violation of the law you order me to be struck?”
text.DRB: Then Paul said to him: God shall strike thee, thou whited wall. And thou sitting judgest me according to the law, and contrary to law doest thou command me to be smitten?
text.Rotherham: Then, Paul, unto him, said—God is about to be smiting thee, thou whited wall! Dost, thou, then sit to judge me according to the law, and, unlawfully, orderest me to be smitten?

text.KJV: And they that stood by said, Revilest thou God’s high priest?
text.NABRE: The attendants said, “Would you revile God’s high priest?”
text.NRSV: Those standing nearby said, “Do you dare to insult God’s high priest?”
text.DRB: And they that stood by, said: Doest thou revile the high Priest of God?
text.Rotherham: And, they who stood by, said—The High-priest of God, dost thou revile?

text.KJV: Then said Paul, I wist not, brethren, that he was the high priest: for it is written, Thou shalt not speak evil of the ruler of thy people.
text.NABRE: Paul answered, “Brothers, I did not realize he was the high priest. For it is written, ‘You shall not curse a ruler of your people.’”
text.NRSV: And Paul said, “I did not realize, brothers, that he was high priest; for it is written, ‘You shall not speak evil of a leader of your people.’”
text.DRB: And Paul said: I knew not, brethren, that he is the high Priest. For it is written: The prince of thy people thou shalt not misspeak.
text.Rotherham: And Paul said—I was not aware, brethren, that he was high-priest; because it is written—Of a ruler of thy people, shalt thou not speak injuriously.

text.KJV: But when Paul perceived that the one part were Sadducees, and the other Pharisees, he cried out in the council, Men and brethren, I am a Pharisee, the son of a Pharisee: of the hope and resurrection of the dead I am called in question.
text.NABRE: Paul was aware that some were Sadducees and some Pharisees, so he called out before the Sanhedrin, “My brothers, I am a Pharisee, the son of Pharisees; [I] am on trial for hope in the resurrection of the dead.”
text.NRSV: When Paul noticed that some were Sadducees and others were Pharisees, he called out in the council, “Brothers, I am a Pharisee, a son of Pharisees. I am on trial concerning the hope of the resurrection of the dead.”
text.DRB: And Paul knowing that the one part was of Sadducees, and the other of Pharisees, he cried out in the Council: Men brethren, I am a Pharisee, the son of Pharisees: of the hope and resurrection of the dead am I judged.
text.Rotherham: But Paul, getting to know that, the one part, were Sadducees and, the other, Pharisees, began to cry aloud in the council—Brethren! I, am, a Pharisee, son of Pharisees:—Concerning a hope, even of a rising again of the dead, am I to be judged.

text.KJV: And when he had so said, there arose a dissension between the Pharisees and the Sadducees: and the multitude was divided.
text.NABRE: When he said this, a dispute broke out between the Pharisees and Sadducees, and the group became divided.
text.NRSV: When he said this, a dissension began between the Pharisees and the Sadducees, and the assembly was divided.
text.DRB: And when he had said these things, there rose dissension between the Pharisees and Sadducees; and the multitude was divided.
text.Rotherham: And, as this he was saying, there arose a dissension of the Pharisees and Sadducees; and rent asunder was the throng.

text.KJV: For the Sadducees say that there is no resurrection, neither angel, nor spirit: but the Pharisees confess both.
text.NABRE: For the Sadducees say that there is no resurrection or angels or spirits, while the Pharisees acknowledge all three.
text.NRSV: (The Sadducees say that there is no resurrection, or angel, or spirit; but the Pharisees acknowledge all three.)
text.DRB: For the Sadducees say there is no resurrection, nor Angel, nor spirit: but the Pharisees confess both.
text.Rotherham: For, Sadducees, say, there is no rising again, nor messenger, nor spirit, whereas, Pharisees, confess them both.

text.KJV: And there arose a great cry: and the scribes that were of the Pharisees’ part arose, and strove, saying, We find no evil in this man: but if a spirit or an angel hath spoken to him, let us not fight against God.
text.NABRE: A great uproar occurred, and some scribes belonging to the Pharisee party stood up and sharply argued, “We find nothing wrong with this man. Suppose a spirit or an angel has spoken to him?”
text.NRSV: Then a great clamor arose, and certain scribes of the Pharisees’ group stood up and contended, “We find nothing wrong with this man. What if a spirit or an angel has spoken to him?”
text.DRB: And there was made a great cry. And certain of the Pharisees rising up, strove saying: We find no evil in this man. What if a spirit hath spoken to him, or an Angel?
text.Rotherham: And there arose a great outcry, and certain of the Scribes of the party of the Pharisees, standing up, began to strive, saying—Nothing bad, find we in this man;—but, if a spirit hath spoken unto him, or a messenger…

text.KJV: And when there arose a great dissension, the chief captain, fearing lest Paul should have been pulled in pieces of them, commanded the soldiers to go down, and to take him by force from among them, and to bring him into the castle.
text.NABRE: The dispute was so serious that the commander, afraid that Paul would be torn to pieces by them, ordered his troops to go down and rescue him from their midst and take him into the compound.
text.NRSV: When the dissension became violent, the tribune, fearing that they would tear Paul to pieces, ordered the soldiers to go down, take him by force, and bring him into the barracks.
text.DRB: And when there was risen great dissension, the Tribune fearing lest Paul should be torn in pieces by them, commanded the soldiers to go down, and to take him out of the midst of them, and to bring him into the castle.
text.Rotherham: And, great dissension arising, the captain, fearing lest Paul would be torn in pieces by them, ordered the troop to go down, and take him by force out of their midst, to bring him into the castle.

text.KJV: And the night following the Lord stood by him, and said, Be of good cheer, Paul: for as thou hast testified of me in Jerusalem, so must thou bear witness also at Rome.
text.NABRE: The following night the Lord stood by him and said, “Take courage. For just as you have borne witness to my cause in Jerusalem, so you must also bear witness in Rome.”
text.NRSV: That night the Lord stood near him and said, “Keep up your courage! For just as you have testified for me in Jerusalem, so you must bear witness also in Rome.”
text.DRB: And the night following our Lord standing by him, said: Be constant; for as thou hast testified of me in Jerusalem, so must thou testify at Rome also.
text.Rotherham: But, on the following night, the Lord, standing over him, said—Be of good courage! for, as thou hast fully borne witness of the things concerning me in Jerusalem, so must thou, in Rome also, bear witness.

text.KJV: And when it was day, certain of the Jews banded together, and bound themselves under a curse, saying that they would neither eat nor drink till they had killed Paul.
text.NABRE: Transfer to Caesarea. When day came, the Jews made a plot and bound themselves by oath not to eat or drink until they had killed Paul.
text.NRSV: In the morning the Jews joined in a conspiracy and bound themselves by an oath neither to eat nor drink until they had killed Paul.
text.DRB: And when day was come, certain of the Jews gathered themselves together, & vowed themselves, saying: that they would neither eat nor drink till they killed Paul.
text.Rotherham: And, when it became day, the Jews, forming a conspiracy, bound themselves under a curse, saying, that they would neither eat nor drink till they had slain Paul.

text.KJV: And they were more than forty which had made this conspiracy.
text.NABRE: There were more than forty who formed this conspiracy.
text.NRSV: There were more than forty who joined in this conspiracy.
text.DRB: And they were more than forty men that had made this conspiracy:
text.Rotherham: And they were, more than forty, who, this sworn-confederacy, had formed.

text.KJV: And they came to the chief priests and elders, and said, We have bound ourselves under a great curse, that we will eat nothing until we have slain Paul.
text.NABRE: They went to the chief priests and elders and said, “We have bound ourselves by a solemn oath to taste nothing until we have killed Paul.
text.NRSV: They went to the chief priests and elders and said, “We have strictly bound ourselves by an oath to taste no food until we have killed Paul.
text.DRB: who came to the chief priests and the ancients, and said: By execration we have vowed ourselves, that we will eat nothing, till we kill Paul.
text.Rotherham: And they went unto the High-priests and Elders, and said—With a curse have we bound ourselves, to taste, nothing, until we have slain Paul.

text.KJV: Now therefore ye with the council signify to the chief captain that he bring him down unto you to morrow, as though ye would enquire something more perfectly concerning him: and we, or ever he come near, are ready to kill him.
text.NABRE: You, together with the Sanhedrin, must now make an official request to the commander to have him bring him down to you, as though you meant to investigate his case more thoroughly. We on our part are prepared to kill him before he arrives.”
text.NRSV: Now then, you and the council must notify the tribune to bring him down to you, on the pretext that you want to make a more thorough examination of his case. And we are ready to do away with him before he arrives.”
text.DRB: Now therefore give you knowledge to the Tribune with the Council, that he bring him forth to you, as if you meant to know some more certainty touching him. But we, before he come near, are ready for to kill him.
text.Rotherham: Now, therefore, do, ye, with the High-council, make it appear unto the captain, that he should bring him down unto you, as though about to ascertain more exactly the things that concern him; and, we, or ever he come near, are ready to kill him.

text.KJV: And when Paul’s sister’s son heard of their lying in wait, he went and entered into the castle, and told Paul.
text.NABRE: The son of Paul’s sister, however, heard about the ambush; so he went and entered the compound and reported it to Paul.
text.NRSV: Now the son of Paul’s sister heard about the ambush; so he went and gained entrance to the barracks and told Paul.
text.DRB: Which when Paul's sister's son had heard, of their lying in wait, he came and entered into the castle and told Paul.
text.Rotherham: But Paul’s sister’s son hearing of the lying-in-wait, happening to be near, and coming into the castle,—reported it unto Paul.

text.KJV: Then Paul called one of the centurions unto him, and said, Bring this young man unto the chief captain: for he hath a certain thing to tell him.
text.NABRE: Paul then called one of the centurions and requested, “Take this young man to the commander; he has something to report to him.”
text.NRSV: Paul called one of the centurions and said, “Take this young man to the tribune, for he has something to report to him.”
text.DRB: And Paul calling to him one of the Centurions, said: Bring this young man to the Tribune, for he hath something to tell him.
text.Rotherham: And Paul, calling unto him one of the centurions, said—This young man, lead thou away unto the captain, for he hath somewhat to report unto him.

text.KJV: So he took him, and brought him to the chief captain, and said, Paul the prisoner called me unto him, and prayed me to bring this young man unto thee, who hath something to say unto thee.
text.NABRE: So he took him and brought him to the commander and explained, “The prisoner Paul called me and asked that I bring this young man to you; he has something to say to you.”
text.NRSV: So he took him, brought him to the tribune, and said, “The prisoner Paul called me and asked me to bring this young man to you; he has something to tell you.”
text.DRB: And he taking him, brought him to the Tribune, and said: The prisoner Paul desired me to bring this young man unto thee, having something to say to thee.
text.Rotherham: He, therefore, taking him with him, brought him unto the captain, and saith—The prisoner Paul, calling me unto him, requested me to bring this young man unto thee, as having somewhat to tell thee.

text.KJV: Then the chief captain took him by the hand, and went with him aside privately, and asked him, What is that thou hast to tell me?
text.NABRE: The commander took him by the hand, drew him aside, and asked him privately, “What is it you have to report to me?”
text.NRSV: The tribune took him by the hand, drew him aside privately, and asked, “What is it that you have to report to me?”
text.DRB: And the Tribune taking him by the hand, went aside with him apart, and asked him: What is it that thou hast to tell me?
text.Rotherham: And the captain, taking him by the hand, and going aside, began, privately, to ask—What is it which thou hast to report unto me?

text.KJV: And he said, The Jews have agreed to desire thee that thou wouldest bring down Paul to morrow into the council, as though they would enquire somewhat of him more perfectly.
text.NABRE: He replied, “The Jews have conspired to ask you to bring Paul down to the Sanhedrin tomorrow, as though they meant to inquire about him more thoroughly,
text.NRSV: He answered, “The Jews have agreed to ask you to bring Paul down to the council tomorrow, as though they were going to inquire more thoroughly into his case.
text.DRB: And he said: The Jews have agreed to desire thee, that tomorrow thou wilt bring forth Paul into the Council, as though they meant to inquire some more certainty touching him.
text.Rotherham: And he said—The Jews, have agreed to request thee, that, to-morrow, thou wouldst bring, Paul, down into the High-council, as though about to ascertain something, more exact, concerning him.

text.KJV: But do not thou yield unto them: for there lie in wait for him of them more than forty men, which have bound themselves with an oath, that they will neither eat nor drink till they have killed him: and now are they ready, looking for a promise from thee.
text.NABRE: but do not believe them. More than forty of them are lying in wait for him; they have bound themselves by oath not to eat or drink until they have killed him. They are now ready and only wait for your consent.”
text.NRSV: But do not be persuaded by them, for more than forty of their men are lying in ambush for him. They have bound themselves by an oath neither to eat nor drink until they kill him. They are ready now and are waiting for your consent.”
text.DRB: But do not thou credit them; for there lie in wait for him more than forty men of them, which have vowed neither to eat nor to drink, till they kill him: and they are now ready, expecting thy promise.
text.Rotherham: Thou, therefore, do not be persuaded by them, for there are lying in wait for him, from among them, more than forty men,—who, indeed, have bound themselves under a curse, neither to eat nor drink, till they have killed him; and, now, are they ready, awaiting the promise, from thee.

text.KJV: So the chief captain then let the young man depart, and charged him, See thou tell no man that thou hast shewed these things to me.
text.NABRE: As the commander dismissed the young man he directed him, “Tell no one that you gave me this information.”
text.NRSV: So the tribune dismissed the young man, ordering him, “Tell no one that you have informed me of this.”
text.DRB: The Tribune therefore dismissed the young man, commanding that he should speak to no man that he had notified these things unto him.
text.Rotherham: the captain, therefore, dismissed the young man, charging him—Unto no one, divulge thou, that, these things, thou hast shewed unto me.

text.KJV: And he called unto him two centurions, saying, Make ready two hundred soldiers to go to Cesarea, and horsemen threescore and ten, and spearmen two hundred, at the third hour of the night;
text.NABRE: Then he summoned two of the centurions and said, “Get two hundred soldiers ready to go to Caesarea by nine o’clock tonight, along with seventy horsemen and two hundred auxiliaries.
text.NRSV: Then he summoned two of the centurions and said, “Get ready to leave by nine o’clock tonight for Caesarea with two hundred soldiers, seventy horsemen, and two hundred spearmen.
text.DRB: And calling two Centurions, he said to them: Make ready two hundred soldiers, to go as far as Cæsarea, and seventy horsemen, and lances two hundred, from the third hour of the night:
text.Rotherham: And calling certain two of the centurions he said—Make ye ready two hundred soldiers, that they may journey as far as Caesarea,—and seventy horsemen, and two hundred spearmen, by the third hour of the night;

text.KJV: And provide them beasts, that they may set Paul on, and bring him safe unto Felix the governor.
text.NABRE: Provide mounts for Paul to ride and give him safe conduct to Felix the governor.”
text.NRSV: Also provide mounts for Paul to ride, and take him safely to Felix the governor.”
text.DRB: and prepare beasts: that setting Paul on, they might bring him safe to Felix the President.
text.Rotherham: beasts also provide, in order that, seating Paul thereon, they may bring him safely through unto Felix the governor.

text.KJV: And he wrote a letter after this manner:
text.NABRE: Then he wrote a letter with this content:
text.NRSV: He wrote a letter to this effect:
text.DRB: (For he feared lest perhaps the Jews might take him away, and kill him, and himself afterward should sustain reproach, as though he would have taken money)
text.Rotherham: And he wrote a letter, after this form:—

text.DRB: writing a letter containing this much: Claudius Lysias to the most excellent President Felix, greeting.
text.NABRE: “Claudius Lysias to his excellency the governor Felix, greetings.
text.KJV: Claudius Lysias unto the most excellent governor Felix sendeth greeting.
text.Rotherham: Claudius Lysias, unto the most excellent governor Felix, Joy!
text.NRSV: “Claudius Lysias to his Excellency the governor Felix, greetings.

text.KJV: This man was taken of the Jews, and should have been killed of them: then came I with an army, and rescued him, having understood that he was a Roman.
text.NABRE: This man, seized by the Jews and about to be murdered by them, I rescued after intervening with my troops when I learned that he was a Roman citizen.
text.NRSV: This man was seized by the Jews and was about to be killed by them, but when I had learned that he was a Roman citizen, I came with the guard and rescued him.
text.DRB: This man being apprehended of the Jews, and ready to be killed of them, I coming in with the band delivered him, understanding that he is a Roman:
text.Rotherham: This man, having been apprehended by the Jews, and being about to be killed by them, I went down with the troop, and rescued; having learned that he was, a Roman.

text.KJV: And when I would have known the cause wherefore they accused him, I brought him forth into their council:
text.NABRE: I wanted to learn the reason for their accusations against him so I brought him down to their Sanhedrin.
text.NRSV: Since I wanted to know the charge for which they accused him, I had him brought to their council.
text.DRB: and meaning to know the cause that they objected unto him, I brought him down into their Council.
text.Rotherham: And, being minded to find out the cause for which they were accusing him, [I took him down into their High-council],

text.KJV: Whom I perceived to be accused of questions of their law, but to have nothing laid to his charge worthy of death or of bonds.
text.NABRE: I discovered that he was accused in matters of controversial questions of their law and not of any charge deserving death or imprisonment.
text.NRSV: I found that he was accused concerning questions of their law, but was charged with nothing deserving death or imprisonment.
text.DRB: Whom I found to be accused concerning questions of their law: but having no crime worthy of death or of bands.
text.Rotherham: whom I found to be accused concerning questions of their law, but, of nothing worthy of death or bonds, to be charged.

text.KJV: And when it was told me how that the Jews laid wait for the man, I sent straightway to thee, and gave commandment to his accusers also to say before thee what they had against him. Farewell.
text.NABRE: Since it was brought to my attention that there will be a plot against the man, I am sending him to you at once, and have also notified his accusers to state [their case] against him before you.”
text.NRSV: When I was informed that there would be a plot against the man, I sent him to you at once, ordering his accusers also to state before you what they have against him. ”
text.DRB: And when it was told me of ambushments that they had prepared against him, I sent him to thee, signifying also to the accusers, to speak before thee. Fare-well.
text.Rotherham: But, when I was informed there would be a plot against the man, forthwith, I sent him unto thee, charging, his accusers also, to be speaking against him before thee.

text.KJV: Then the soldiers, as it was commanded them, took Paul, and brought him by night to Antipatris.
text.NABRE: So the soldiers, according to their orders, took Paul and escorted him by night to Antipatris.
text.NRSV: So the soldiers, according to their instructions, took Paul and brought him during the night to Antipatris.
text.DRB: And the soldiers according as it was commanded them, taking Paul, brought him by night to Antipatris.
text.Rotherham: So the soldiers, according to their orders, taking up Paul, brought him by night unto Antipatris;

text.KJV: On the morrow they left the horsemen to go with him, and returned to the castle:
text.NABRE: The next day they returned to the compound, leaving the horsemen to complete the journey with him.
text.NRSV: The next day they let the horsemen go on with him, while they returned to the barracks.
text.DRB: And the next day sending away the horse-men to go with him, they returned to the castle.
text.Rotherham: and, on the morrow, leaving the horsemen to go on with him, returned to the castle,—

text.KJV: Who, when they came to Cesarea, and delivered the epistle to the governor, presented Paul also before him.
text.NABRE: When they arrived in Caesarea they delivered the letter to the governor and presented Paul to him.
text.NRSV: When they came to Caesarea and delivered the letter to the governor, they presented Paul also before him.
text.DRB: Who when they were come to Cæsarea, and had delivered the letter to the President, they did set Paul also before him.
text.Rotherham: and the others, entering into Caesarea, and delivering the letter unto the governor, set, Paul also, before him.

text.KJV: And when the governor had read the letter, he asked of what province he was. And when he understood that he was of Cilicia;
text.NABRE: When he had read it and asked to what province he belonged, and learned that he was from Cilicia,
text.NRSV: On reading the letter, he asked what province he belonged to, and when he learned that he was from Cilicia,
text.DRB: And when he had read, and had asked of what province he was: and understanding that of Cilicia:
text.Rotherham: And, when he had read it, and asked out of what province he was, and learned that he was from Cilicia,

text.KJV: I will hear thee, said he, when thine accusers are also come. And he commanded him to be kept in Herod’s judgment hall.
text.NABRE: he said, “I shall hear your case when your accusers arrive.” Then he ordered that he be held in custody in Herod’s praetorium.
text.NRSV: he said, “I will give you a hearing when your accusers arrive.” Then he ordered that he be kept under guard in Herod’s headquarters.
text.DRB: I will hear thee, said he, when thy accusers are come. And he commanded him to be kept in Herod's palace.
text.Rotherham: I myself will hear thee in full, said he, whensoever, thine accusers also, are come; and gave orders that, in the palace of Herod, he should be kept under guard.

text.KJV: And after five days Ananias the high priest descended with the elders, and with a certain orator named Tertullus, who informed the governor against Paul.
text.NABRE: Five days later the high priest Ananias came down with some elders and an advocate, a certain Tertullus, and they presented formal charges against Paul to the governor.
text.NRSV: Five days later the high priest Ananias came down with some elders and an attorney, a certain Tertullus, and they reported their case against Paul to the governor.
text.DRB: And after five days the high priest Ananias descended, with certain ancients and one Tertullus an orator, who went to the President against Paul.
text.Rotherham: And, after five days, came down the High-priest Ananias, with certain Elders and a certain orator Tertullus, and they informed the governor against Paul.

text.KJV: And when he was called forth, Tertullus began to accuse him, saying, Seeing that by thee we enjoy great quietness, and that very worthy deeds are done unto this nation by thy providence,
text.NABRE: When he was called, Tertullus began to accuse him, saying, “Since we have attained much peace through you, and reforms have been accomplished in this nation through your provident care,
text.NRSV: When Paul had been summoned, Tertullus began to accuse him, saying: “Your Excellency, because of you we have long enjoyed peace, and reforms have been made for this people because of your foresight.
text.DRB: And Paul being cited, Tertullus began to accuse, saying: Whereas we live in much peace by thee, & many things are corrected by thy providence,
text.Rotherham: And, when he was called, Tertullus began to make accusation, saying—Seeing that, great peace, we are obtaining through thee, and that, reforms, are being brought about for this nation through thy forethought,

text.KJV: We accept it always, and in all places, most noble Felix, with all thankfulness.
text.NABRE: we acknowledge this in every way and everywhere, most excellent Felix, with all gratitude.
text.NRSV: We welcome this in every way and everywhere with utmost gratitude.
text.DRB: we do always & in all places receive it, most excellent Felix, with all thanks-giving.
text.Rotherham: both in all ways and in all places, are we accepting it, most excellent Felix, with all thankfulness.

text.KJV: Notwithstanding, that I be not further tedious unto thee, I pray thee that thou wouldest hear us of thy clemency a few words.
text.NABRE: But in order not to detain you further, I ask you to give us a brief hearing with your customary graciousness.
text.NRSV: But, to detain you no further, I beg you to hear us briefly with your customary graciousness.
text.DRB: But lest I hinder thee any longer, I desire thee of thy clemency briefly to hear us.
text.Rotherham: But, lest I too long detain thee, I beseech thee to hear us concisely in thy considerateness.

text.KJV: For we have found this man a pestilent fellow, and a mover of sedition among all the Jews throughout the world, and a ringleader of the sect of the Nazarenes:
text.NABRE: We found this man to be a pest; he creates dissension among Jews all over the world and is a ringleader of the sect of the Nazoreans.
text.NRSV: We have, in fact, found this man a pestilent fellow, an agitator among all the Jews throughout the world, and a ringleader of the sect of the Nazarenes.
text.DRB: We have found this man pestiferous, & raising seditions to all the Jews in the whole world, and author of the sedition of the sect of the Nazarenes.
text.Rotherham: For, finding this man a pest, and moving sedition with all the Jews that are throughout the inhabited earth, a leader also of the sect of the Nazarenes,—

text.KJV: Who also hath gone about to profane the temple: whom we took, and would have judged according to our law.
text.NABRE: He even tried to desecrate our temple, but we arrested him.
text.NRSV: He even tried to profane the temple, and so we seized him.
text.DRB: Who also hath attempted to violate the temple, whom also being apprehended we would have judged according to our law.
text.Rotherham: who also attempted to desecrate even, the temple, whom we also seized,

text.KJV: But the chief captain Lysias came upon us, and with great violence took him away out of our hands,
text.DRB: But Lysias the Tribune coming in, with great force took him away out of our hands,

text.KJV: Commanding his accusers to come unto thee: by examining of whom thyself mayest take knowledge of all these things, whereof we accuse him.
text.NABRE: If you examine him you will be able to learn from him for yourself about everything of which we are accusing him.”
text.NRSV: By examining him yourself you will be able to learn from him concerning everything of which we accuse him.”
text.DRB: commanding his accusers to come to thee, of whom thou mayest thyself judging, understand of all these things, whereof we accuse him.
text.Rotherham: from whom thou shall be able, thyself, by making examination concerning all these things, to ascertain the things of which, we, are accusing him.

text.KJV: And the Jews also assented, saying that these things were so.
text.NABRE: The Jews also joined in the attack and asserted that these things were so.
text.NRSV: The Jews also joined in the charge by asserting that all this was true.
text.DRB: And the Jews also added, saying that these things were so.
text.Rotherham: Moreover, the Jews also were joining in the attack, saying that, these things, were, so.

text.KJV: Then Paul, after that the governor had beckoned unto him to speak, answered, Forasmuch as I know that thou hast been of many years a judge unto this nation, I do the more cheerfully answer for myself:
text.NABRE: Then the governor motioned to him to speak and Paul replied, “I know that you have been a judge over this nation for many years and so I am pleased to make my defense before you.
text.NRSV: When the governor motioned to him to speak, Paul replied: “I cheerfully make my defense, knowing that for many years you have been a judge over this nation.
text.DRB: But Paul answered: (the President making a sign unto him for to speak) Knowing that of many years thou art judge over this nation, I will with good courage answer for myself.
text.Rotherham: And Paul answered, when the governor had motioned him to be speaking,—Well knowing thee to have been, for many years, judge unto his nation, cheerfully, as to the things concerning myself, do I make defence;

text.KJV: Because that thou mayest understand, that there are yet but twelve days since I went up to Jerusalem for to worship.
text.NABRE: As you can verify, not more than twelve days have passed since I went up to Jerusalem to worship.
text.NRSV: As you can find out, it is not more than twelve days since I went up to worship in Jerusalem.
text.DRB: For thou mayest understand that it is not above twelve days to me, since I went up to adore in Jerusalem.
text.Rotherham: seeing thou art able to ascertain, that there are, not more, than twelve days, since I went up to worship in Jerusalem,—

text.KJV: And they neither found me in the temple disputing with any man, neither raising up the people, neither in the synagogues, nor in the city:
text.NABRE: Neither in the temple, nor in the synagogues, nor anywhere in the city did they find me arguing with anyone or instigating a riot among the people.
text.NRSV: They did not find me disputing with anyone in the temple or stirring up a crowd either in the synagogues or throughout the city.
text.DRB: And neither in the temple did they find me disputing with any man, or causing concourse of the multitude, neither in the synagogues, nor in the city:
text.Rotherham: and neither, in the temple, found they me, with any one, disputing, or causing, a halt, of the multitude, either in the synagogues or throughout the city,—

text.KJV: Neither can they prove the things whereof they now accuse me.
text.NABRE: Nor can they prove to you the accusations they are now making against me.
text.NRSV: Neither can they prove to you the charge that they now bring against me.
text.DRB: neither can they prove unto thee the things whereof they now accuse me.
text.Rotherham: neither can they make good the things concerning which they are, now, accusing me.

text.KJV: But this I confess unto thee, that after the way which they call heresy, so worship I the God of my fathers, believing all things which are written in the law and in the prophets:
text.NABRE: But this I do admit to you, that according to the Way, which they call a sect, I worship the God of our ancestors and I believe everything that is in accordance with the law and written in the prophets.
text.NRSV: But this I admit to you, that according to the Way, which they call a sect, I worship the God of our ancestors, believing everything laid down according to the law or written in the prophets.
text.DRB: But this I confess to thee, that according to the sect, which they call heresy, I do so serve the Father my God, believing all things that are written in the Law & the Prophets:
text.Rotherham: But I confess, this, unto thee,—That, according to the Way which they call a Sect, so, I am rendering divine service unto my father’s God, believing in all the things which, throughout the law, and those which, in the prophets, are written:

text.KJV: And have hope toward God, which they themselves also allow, that there shall be a resurrection of the dead, both of the just and unjust.
text.NABRE: I have the same hope in God as they themselves have that there will be a resurrection of the righteous and the unrighteous.
text.NRSV: I have a hope in God—a hope that they themselves also accept—that there will be a resurrection of both the righteous and the unrighteous.
text.DRB: having hope in God, the which these also themselves expect, that there shall be a resurrection of just and unjust.
text.Rotherham: Having, hope, towards God, which, even these themselves, do entertain—that, a resurrection, there shall certainly be, both of righteous and of unrighteous:

text.KJV: And herein do I exercise myself, to have always a conscience void of offence toward God, and toward men.
text.NABRE: Because of this, I always strive to keep my conscience clear before God and man.
text.NRSV: Therefore I do my best always to have a clear conscience toward God and all people.
text.DRB: In this myself also do study to have a conscience without offence toward God and toward men always.
text.Rotherham: herein, even I, myself, am studying to have, an unoffending conscience, towards God and men, continually.

text.KJV: Now after many years I came to bring alms to my nation, and offerings.
text.NABRE: After many years, I came to bring alms for my nation and offerings.
text.NRSV: Now after some years I came to bring alms to my nation and to offer sacrifices.
text.DRB: And after many years I came to bestow alms upon my nation, & oblations, and vows.
text.Rotherham: Now, after many years, intending to do, alms, unto my nation, I arrived,—also [to present] offerings; among which they found me, purified in the temple, not with a multitude, nor with tumult;

text.KJV: Whereupon certain Jews from Asia found me purified in the temple, neither with multitude, nor with tumult.
text.NABRE: While I was so engaged, they found me, after my purification, in the temple without a crowd or disturbance.
text.NRSV: While I was doing this, they found me in the temple, completing the rite of purification, without any crowd or disturbance.
text.DRB: In the which they found me purified in the temple: not with multitude nor with tumult.
text.Rotherham: but certain Jews from Asia [caused it],—

text.KJV: Who ought to have been here before thee, and object, if they had ought against me.
text.NABRE: But some Jews from the province of Asia, who should be here before you to make whatever accusation they might have against me—
text.NRSV: But there were some Jews from Asia—they ought to be here before you to make an accusation, if they have anything against me.
text.DRB: But certain Jews of Asia, who ought to be present before thee and to accuse, if they had any thing against me:
text.Rotherham: who ought, before thee, to have presented themselves, and to have been laying accusation, if, anything, they might have had against me:—

text.KJV: Or else let these same here say, if they have found any evil doing in me, while I stood before the council,
text.NABRE: or let these men themselves state what crime they discovered when I stood before the Sanhedrin,
text.NRSV: Or let these men here tell what crime they had found when I stood before the council,
text.DRB: or let these men themselves say, if they have found in me any iniquity, forasmuch as I stand in the Council,
text.Rotherham: Or, let, these themselves, say what wrong they found, when I stood before the High-council,—

text.KJV: Except it be for this one voice, that I cried standing among them, Touching the resurrection of the dead I am called in question by you this day.
text.NABRE: unless it was my one outcry as I stood among them, that ‘I am on trial before you today for the resurrection of the dead.’”
text.NRSV: unless it was this one sentence that I called out while standing before them, ‘It is about the resurrection of the dead that I am on trial before you today.’”
text.DRB: but of this one voice only that I cried standing among them, That of the resurrection of the dead am I judged this day of you.
text.Rotherham: unless concerning this one voice, wherewith I cried aloud among them, as I stood—Concerning the raising of the dead, am, I, to be judged, this day, by you.

text.KJV: And when Felix heard these things, having more perfect knowledge of that way, he deferred them, and said, When Lysias the chief captain shall come down, I will know the uttermost of your matter.
text.NABRE: Then Felix, who was accurately informed about the Way, postponed the trial, saying, “When Lysias the commander comes down, I shall decide your case.”
text.NRSV: But Felix, who was rather well informed about the Way, adjourned the hearing with the comment, “When Lysias the tribune comes down, I will decide your case.”
text.DRB: And Felix deferred them, knowing most certainly of this way, saying: When Lysias the Tribune is come down, I will hear you.
text.Rotherham: And Felix deferred them, having more exact knowledge concerning the Way,—saying—As soon as, Lysias the captain, hath come down, I will give judgment as to your affairs,—

text.KJV: And he commanded a centurion to keep Paul, and to let him have liberty, and that he should forbid none of his acquaintance to minister or come unto him.
text.NABRE: He gave orders to the centurion that he should be kept in custody but have some liberty, and that he should not prevent any of his friends from caring for his needs.
text.NRSV: Then he ordered the centurion to keep him in custody, but to let him have some liberty and not to prevent any of his friends from taking care of his needs.
text.DRB: And he commanded the Centurion to keep him, and that he should have rest, neither to prohibit any of his to minister unto him.
text.Rotherham: giving orders unto the centurion, that he should be kept, and have a measure of liberty, and to be hindering, none, of his own from waiting upon him.

text.KJV: And after certain days, when Felix came with his wife Drusilla, which was a Jewess, he sent for Paul, and heard him concerning the faith in Christ.
text.NABRE: Captivity in Caesarea. Several days later Felix came with his wife Drusilla, who was Jewish. He had Paul summoned and listened to him speak about faith in Christ Jesus.
text.NRSV: Some days later when Felix came with his wife Drusilla, who was Jewish, he sent for Paul and heard him speak concerning faith in Christ Jesus.
text.DRB: And after some days, Felix coming with Drusilla his wife, which was a Jew, called Paul, and heard of him the faith that is in Christ Jesus.
text.Rotherham: And, after certain days, Felix having arrived, with Drusilla his own wife, who was, a Jewess, he sent for Paul, and heard him concerning the faith, respecting Christ Jesus.

text.KJV: And as he reasoned of righteousness, temperance, and judgment to come, Felix trembled, and answered, Go thy way for this time; when I have a convenient season, I will call for thee.
text.NABRE: But as he spoke about righteousness and self-restraint and the coming judgment, Felix became frightened and said, “You may go for now; when I find an opportunity I shall summon you again.”
text.NRSV: And as he discussed justice, self-control, and the coming judgment, Felix became frightened and said, “Go away for the present; when I have an opportunity, I will send for you.”
text.DRB: And he disputing of justice and chastity, and of the judgement to come, Felix being terrified, answered: For this time, go thy way: but in time convenient I will send for thee.
text.Rotherham: And, as he was reasoning of righteousness, and self-control, and the judgment to come, Felix, becoming greatly afraid, answered—For the present, be going thy way, and, when I find an opportunity, I will send for thee,—

text.KJV: He hoped also that money should have been given him of Paul, that he might loose him: wherefore he sent for him the oftener, and communed with him.
text.NABRE: At the same time he hoped that a bribe would be offered him by Paul, and so he sent for him very often and conversed with him.
text.NRSV: At the same time he hoped that money would be given him by Paul, and for that reason he used to send for him very often and converse with him.
text.DRB: hoping also withal, that money would be given him of Paul; for the which cause also oftentimes sending for him, he spake with him.
text.Rotherham: at the same time, also hoping that, money, would be given him by Paul; wherefore also, the more frequently sending for him, he used to converse with him.

text.KJV: But after two years Porcius Festus came into Felix’ room: and Felix, willing to shew the Jews a pleasure, left Paul bound.
text.NABRE: Two years passed and Felix was succeeded by Porcius Festus. Wishing to ingratiate himself with the Jews, Felix left Paul in prison.
text.NRSV: After two years had passed, Felix was succeeded by Porcius Festus; and since he wanted to grant the Jews a favor, Felix left Paul in prison.
text.DRB: But when two years were ended, Felix had a Successor Portius Festus. And Felix being willing to shew the Jews a pleasure, left Paul in prison.
text.Rotherham: When, however, two years, were completed, Felix, was succeeded, by Porcius Festus, and Felix, wishing to gain favour with the Jews, left Paul bound.

text.NRSV: Three days after Festus had arrived in the province, he went up from Caesarea to Jerusalem
text.Rotherham: Festus, therefore, having come upon the province, after three days, went up unto Jerusalem from Caesarea;
text.KJV: Now when Festus was come into the province, after three days he ascended from Cesarea to Jerusalem.
text.NABRE: Three days after his arrival in the province, Festus went up from Caesarea to Jerusalem
text.DRB: Festus therefore when he was come into the province, after three days went up to Jerusalem from Cæsarea.

text.NRSV: where the chief priests and the leaders of the Jews gave him a report against Paul. They appealed to him
text.Rotherham: and the High-priest and chiefs of the Jews laid information before him against Paul, and began to beseech him
text.KJV: Then the high priest and the chief of the Jews informed him against Paul, and besought him,
text.NABRE: where the chief priests and Jewish leaders presented him their formal charges against Paul. They asked him
text.DRB: And the chief Priests, and principal men of the Jews went unto him against Paul: and they desired him,

text.NRSV: and requested, as a favor to them against Paul, to have him transferred to Jerusalem. They were, in fact, planning an ambush to kill him along the way.
text.Rotherham: asking for themselves as a favour against him, that he would send for him unto Jerusalem,—making, an ambush, to kill him on the way.
text.KJV: And desired favour against him, that he would send for him to Jerusalem, laying wait in the way to kill him.
text.NABRE: as a favor to have him sent to Jerusalem, for they were plotting to kill him along the way.
text.DRB: requesting favour against him, that he would command him to be brought to Jerusalem, laying wait for to kill him in the way.

text.NRSV: Festus replied that Paul was being kept at Caesarea, and that he himself intended to go there shortly.
text.Rotherham: Festus, therefore, answered, that Paul should be kept in Caesarea, and that, he himself, was about, shortly, to be going out [thither].
text.KJV: But Festus answered, that Paul should be kept at Cesarea, and that he himself would depart shortly thither.
text.NABRE: Festus replied that Paul was being held in custody in Caesarea and that he himself would be returning there shortly.
text.DRB: But Festus answered, that Paul is kept in Cæsarea: and that he would very shortly go thither.

text.NRSV: “So,” he said, “let those of you who have the authority come down with me, and if there is anything wrong about the man, let them accuse him.”
text.Rotherham: They, therefore, among you (saith he) who are in power, let them go down with me; and, if there is in the man, anything amiss, let them accuse him.
text.KJV: Let them therefore, said he, which among you are able, go down with me, and accuse this man, if there be any wickedness in him.
text.NABRE: He said, “Let your authorities come down with me, and if this man has done something improper, let them accuse him.”
text.DRB: They therefore, saith he, that are of ability among you, going down with me, if there be any crime in the man, let them accuse him.

text.NRSV: After he had stayed among them not more than eight or ten days, he went down to Caesarea; the next day he took his seat on the tribunal and ordered Paul to be brought.
text.Rotherham: And, spending among them, not more than eight or ten days, he went down unto Caesarea; and, on the morrow, taking his place upon the judgment-seat, ordered Paul to be brought.
text.KJV: And when he had tarried among them more than ten days, he went down unto Cesarea; and the next day sitting on the judgment seat commanded Paul to be brought.
text.NABRE: After spending no more than eight or ten days with them, he went down to Caesarea, and on the following day took his seat on the tribunal and ordered that Paul be brought in.
text.DRB: And having tarried among them not above eight or ten days, he went down to Cæsarea, and the next day he sat in the judgement seat: and he commanded Paul to be brought.

text.NRSV: When he arrived, the Jews who had gone down from Jerusalem surrounded him, bringing many serious charges against him, which they could not prove.
text.Rotherham: And, when he presented himself, the Jews who, from Jerusalem, had come down, stood round about him, many and grievous charges, bringing against [him], which they were not able to prove,—
text.KJV: And when he was come, the Jews which came down from Jerusalem stood round about, and laid many and grievous complaints against Paul, which they could not prove.
text.NABRE: When he appeared, the Jews who had come down from Jerusalem surrounded him and brought many serious charges against him, which they were unable to prove.
text.DRB: Who being brought, there stood about him the Jews that were come down from Jerusalem, objecting many and grievous causes which they could not prove;

text.NRSV: Paul said in his defense, “I have in no way committed an offense against the law of the Jews, or against the temple, or against the emperor.”
text.Rotherham: Paul saying in defence—Neither against the law of the Jews, nor against the temple, nor against Caesar, have I in anything sinned.
text.KJV: While he answered for himself, Neither against the law of the Jews, neither against the temple, nor yet against Cesar, have I offended any thing at all.
text.NABRE: In defending himself Paul said, “I have committed no crime either against the Jewish law or against the temple or against Caesar.”
text.DRB: Paul making answer, That neither against the law of the Jews, nor against the temple, nor against Cæsar have I any thing offended.

text.NRSV: But Festus, wishing to do the Jews a favor, asked Paul, “Do you wish to go up to Jerusalem and be tried there before me on these charges?”
text.Rotherham: But, Festus, wishing, with the Jews, to gain, favour, answered Paul, and said—Art thou willing, unto Jerusalem, to go up, and, there, concerning these things, be judged before me?
text.KJV: But Festus, willing to do the Jews a pleasure, answered Paul, and said, Wilt thou go up to Jerusalem, and there be judged of these things before me?
text.NABRE: Then Festus, wishing to ingratiate himself with the Jews, said to Paul in reply, “Are you willing to go up to Jerusalem and there stand trial before me on these charges?”
text.DRB: But Festus willing to shew the Jews a pleasure, answering Paul, said: Wilt thou go up to Jerusalem, and there be judged of these things before me?

text.NRSV: Paul said, “I am appealing to the emperor’s tribunal; this is where I should be tried. I have done no wrong to the Jews, as you very well know.
text.Rotherham: But Paul said—Standing before the judgment-seat of Caesar, am I, where, I, ought to be judged. Unto the Jews, have I done no wrong, as, even thou, right well, art discovering.
text.KJV: Then said Paul, I stand at Cesar’s judgment seat, where I ought to be judged: to the Jews have I done no wrong, as thou very well knowest.
text.NABRE: Paul answered, “I am standing before the tribunal of Caesar; this is where I should be tried. I have committed no crime against the Jews, as you very well know.
text.DRB: And Paul said: At Cæsar's judgement seat do I stand, where I ought to be judged: the Jews I have not hurt, as thou very well knowest.

text.NRSV: Now if I am in the wrong and have committed something for which I deserve to die, I am not trying to escape death; but if there is nothing to their charges against me, no one can turn me over to them. I appeal to the emperor.”
text.Rotherham: If then, on the one hand, I am doing wrong, and, anything worthy of death, have committed, I excuse not myself from dying; but, on the other hand, if there is, nothing, in the things whereof these are accusing me, no man, hath power to give, me, unto them as a favour:—Unto Caesar, I appeal!
text.KJV: For if I be an offender, or have committed any thing worthy of death, I refuse not to die: but if there be none of these things whereof these accuse me, no man may deliver me unto them. I appeal unto Cesar.
text.NABRE: If I have committed a crime or done anything deserving death, I do not seek to escape the death penalty; but if there is no substance to the charges they are bringing against me, then no one has the right to hand me over to them. I appeal to Caesar.”
text.DRB: For if I have hurt them, or done any thing worthy of death, I refuse not to die. But if none of those things be, whereof these accuse me, no man can give me to them. I appeal to Cæsar.

text.NRSV: Then Festus, after he had conferred with his council, replied, “You have appealed to the emperor; to the emperor you will go.”
text.Rotherham: Then Festus, having conversed with the council, answered—Unto Caesar, hast thou appealed? Unto Caesar, shalt thou go.
text.KJV: Then Festus, when he had conferred with the council, answered, Hast thou appealed unto Cesar? unto Cesar shalt thou go.
text.NABRE: Then Festus, after conferring with his council, replied, “You have appealed to Caesar. To Caesar you will go.”
text.DRB: Then Festus having conferred with the Council, answered: Hast thou appealed to Cæsar? to Cæsar shalt thou go.

text.NRSV: After several days had passed, King Agrippa and Bernice arrived at Caesarea to welcome Festus.
text.Rotherham: And, some days having gone by, Agrippa the king and Bernice came down to Caesarea, to salute Festus.
text.KJV: And after certain days king Agrippa and Bernice came unto Cesarea to salute Festus.
text.NABRE: Paul Before King Agrippa. When a few days had passed, King Agrippa and Bernice arrived in Caesarea on a visit to Festus.
text.DRB: And when certain days were passed, king Agrippa & Bernice came down to Cæsarea to salute Festus.

text.NRSV: Since they were staying there several days, Festus laid Paul’s case before the king, saying, “There is a man here who was left in prison by Felix.
text.Rotherham: And, as they were spending more days there, Festus, repeated, unto the king, the things relating to Paul, saying—A certain man, hath been left behind by Felix, as a prisoner;
text.KJV: And when they had been there many days, Festus declared Paul’s cause unto the king, saying, There is a certain man left in bonds by Felix:
text.NABRE: Since they spent several days there, Festus referred Paul’s case to the king, saying, “There is a man here left in custody by Felix.
text.DRB: And as they tarried there a good many days, Festus signified to the king, of Paul, saying: A certain person was left prisoner by Felix,

text.NRSV: When I was in Jerusalem, the chief priests and the elders of the Jews informed me about him and asked for a sentence against him.
text.Rotherham: concerning whom, when I happened to be in Jerusalem, the High-priests and the Elders of the Jews laid information, claiming against him a condemnation:
text.KJV: About whom, when I was at Jerusalem, the chief priests and the elders of the Jews informed me, desiring to have judgment against him.
text.NABRE: When I was in Jerusalem the chief priests and the elders of the Jews brought charges against him and demanded his condemnation.
text.DRB: concerning whom, when I was at Jerusalem, the chief Priests and the Ancients of the Jews came unto me, desiring condemnation against him.

text.NRSV: I told them that it was not the custom of the Romans to hand over anyone before the accused had met the accusers face to face and had been given an opportunity to make a defense against the charge.
text.Rotherham: unto whom made answer—That it is not a custom with Romans, to grant as a favour any man, before the accused, face to face, should have his accusers, and, opportunity of defence, should receive, concerning the charge.
text.KJV: To whom I answered, It is not the manner of the Romans to deliver any man to die, before that he which is accused have the accusers face to face, and have licence to answer for himself concerning the crime laid against him.
text.NABRE: I answered them that it was not Roman practice to hand over an accused person before he has faced his accusers and had the opportunity to defend himself against their charge.
text.DRB: To whom I answered: That it is not the Romans' custom to yield up any man before that he which is accused have his accusers present, and take place to make his answer for to clear himself of the crimes.

text.NRSV: So when they met here, I lost no time, but on the next day took my seat on the tribunal and ordered the man to be brought.
text.Rotherham: When, therefore, they had come together here, no delay whatever, making, on the next day, taking my place upon the judgment-seat, I ordered the man to be brought:
text.KJV: Therefore, when they were come hither, without any delay on the morrow I sat on the judgment seat, and commanded the man to be brought forth.
text.NABRE: So when [they] came together here, I made no delay; the next day I took my seat on the tribunal and ordered the man to be brought in.
text.DRB: When they therefore were assembled hither, without any delay, the day following, sitting in the judgement seat, I commanded the man to be brought.

text.NRSV: When the accusers stood up, they did not charge him with any of the crimes that I was expecting.
text.Rotherham: Concerning whom, taking their stand, his accusers, no accusation at all, were bringing, of the evil things which, I, had been suspecting;
text.KJV: Against whom when the accusers stood up, they brought none accusation of such things as I supposed:
text.NABRE: His accusers stood around him, but did not charge him with any of the crimes I suspected.
text.DRB: Of whom, when the accusers stood up, they brought no cause which I thought ill of:

text.NRSV: Instead they had certain points of disagreement with him about their own religion and about a certain Jesus, who had died, but whom Paul asserted to be alive.
text.Rotherham: but, certain questions concerning their own demon-worship, had they against him, and concerning one Jesus, who had died, whom Paul was affirming to be alive.
text.KJV: But had certain questions against him of their own superstition, and of one Jesus, which was dead, whom Paul affirmed to be alive.
text.NABRE: Instead they had some issues with him about their own religion and about a certain Jesus who had died but who Paul claimed was alive.
text.DRB: but certain questions of their own superstition they had against him, and of one Jesus deceased, whom Paul affirmed to live.

text.NRSV: Since I was at a loss how to investigate these questions, I asked whether he wished to go to Jerusalem and be tried there on these charges.
text.Rotherham: And, I, being at a loss as to the inquiry into these things, was asking—whether he might be minded to go unto Jerusalem, and, there, be judged concerning these things.
text.KJV: And because I doubted of such manner of questions, I asked him whether he would go to Jerusalem, and there be judged of these matters.
text.NABRE: Since I was at a loss how to investigate this controversy, I asked if he were willing to go to Jerusalem and there stand trial on these charges.
text.DRB: Doubting therefore of this kind of question, I said, whether he would go to Jerusalem, & there be judged of these things.

text.NRSV: But when Paul had appealed to be kept in custody for the decision of his Imperial Majesty, I ordered him to be held until I could send him to the emperor.”
text.Rotherham: But, Paul, having appealed to be kept for the decision of the Emperor, I ordered him to be kept, until I could send him up unto Caesar.
text.KJV: But when Paul had appealed to be reserved unto the hearing of Augustus, I commanded him to be kept till I might send him to Cesar.
text.NABRE: And when Paul appealed that he be held in custody for the Emperor’s decision, I ordered him held until I could send him to Caesar.”
text.DRB: But Paul appealing to be kept unto the knowledge of Augustus, I commanded him to be kept, till I send him to Cæsar.

text.NRSV: Agrippa said to Festus, “I would like to hear the man myself.” “Tomorrow,” he said, “you will hear him.”
text.Rotherham: And, Agrippa, [said] unto Festus—I could wish, myself also, to hear, the man. To-morrow, (saith he) thou shalt hear him.
text.KJV: Then Agrippa said unto Festus, I would also hear the man myself. To morrow, said he, thou shalt hear him.
text.NABRE: Agrippa said to Festus, “I too should like to hear this man.” He replied, “Tomorrow you will hear him.”
text.DRB: And Agrippa said to Festus: Myself also would hear the man. Tomorrow, said he, thou shalt hear him.

text.NRSV: So on the next day Agrippa and Bernice came with great pomp, and they entered the audience hall with the military tribunes and the prominent men of the city. Then Festus gave the order and Paul was brought in.
text.Rotherham: On the morrow, therefore, when Agrippa had come, and Bernice, with great display, and they had entered into the audience-chamber, with the captains of thousands and men of distinction of the city,—and Festus had given orders, Paul was brought.
text.KJV: And on the morrow, when Agrippa was come, and Bernice, with great pomp, and was entered into the place of hearing, with the chief captains, and principal men of the city, at Festus’ commandment Paul was brought forth.
text.NABRE: The next day Agrippa and Bernice came with great ceremony and entered the audience hall in the company of cohort commanders and the prominent men of the city and, by command of Festus, Paul was brought in.
text.DRB: And the next day when Agrippa and Bernice were come with great pomp, and had entered into the hall of audience with the Tribunes and principal men of the city, at Festus' commandment Paul was brought.

text.NRSV: And Festus said, “King Agrippa and all here present with us, you see this man about whom the whole Jewish community petitioned me, both in Jerusalem and here, shouting that he ought not to live any longer.
text.Rotherham: And Festus saith—King Agrippa! and all ye men, here present with us: Ye observe this person, concerning whom, one and all the throng of the Jews, have interceded with me, both in Jerusalem and here, crying aloud that he ought not to be living any longer.
text.KJV: And Festus said, King Agrippa, and all men which are here present with us, ye see this man, about whom all the multitude of the Jews have dealt with me, both at Jerusalem, and also here, crying that he ought not to live any longer.
text.NABRE: And Festus said, “King Agrippa and all you here present with us, look at this man about whom the whole Jewish populace petitioned me here and in Jerusalem, clamoring that he should live no longer.
text.DRB: And Festus saith: King Agrippa, and all ye men that are present together with us, you see this man, concerning whom all the multitude of the Jews called upon me at Jerusalem, requesting and crying out that he ought not to live any longer.

text.NRSV: But I found that he had done nothing deserving death; and when he appealed to his Imperial Majesty, I decided to send him.
text.Rotherham: But, I, gathered, that, nothing worthy of death, had he committed; and, this man himself, having appealed unto the Emperor, I decided to send him:—
text.KJV: But when I found that he had committed nothing worthy of death, and that he himself hath appealed to Augustus, I have determined to send him.
text.NABRE: I found, however, that he had done nothing deserving death, and so when he appealed to the Emperor, I decided to send him.
text.DRB: Yet have I found nothing that he hath committed worthy of death. But forasmuch as he himself appealed to Augustus, I have determined to send him.

text.NRSV: But I have nothing definite to write to our sovereign about him. Therefore I have brought him before all of you, and especially before you, King Agrippa, so that, after we have examined him, I may have something to write—
text.Rotherham: Concerning whom, anything certain to write unto my lord, I have not; wherefore, I have brought him forth before you,—and especially before thee, King Agrippa! in order that, after examination had, I might have something I could write;
text.KJV: Of whom I have no certain thing to write unto my lord. Wherefore I have brought him forth before you, and specially before thee, O king Agrippa, that, after examination had, I might have somewhat to write.
text.NABRE: But I have nothing definite to write about him to our sovereign; therefore I have brought him before all of you, and particularly before you, King Agrippa, so that I may have something to write as a result of this investigation.
text.DRB: Of whom what to write for certainty to my lord, I have not. For the which cause I have brought him forth to you, and especially to thee, king Agrippa, that examination being made, I may have what to write.

text.NRSV: for it seems to me unreasonable to send a prisoner without indicating the charges against him.”
text.Rotherham: For, unreasonable, unto me, it seemeth, when sending a prisoner, not also, the accusations against him, to signify.
text.KJV: For it seemeth to me unreasonable to send a prisoner, and not withal to signify the crimes laid against him.
text.NABRE: For it seems senseless to me to send up a prisoner without indicating the charges against him.”
text.DRB: For it seemeth to me without reason, to send a prisoner, & not to signify his causes.

text.NRSV: Agrippa said to Paul, “You have permission to speak for yourself.” Then Paul stretched out his hand and began to defend himself:
text.Rotherham: And, Agrippa, to Paul, said—It is permitted thee, on thine own behalf, to be speaking. Then Paul, stretching forth his hand, went on to make his defence.
text.KJV: Then Agrippa said unto Paul, Thou art permitted to speak for thyself. Then Paul stretched forth the hand, and answered for himself:
text.NABRE: Then Agrippa said to Paul, “You may now speak on your own behalf.” So Paul stretched out his hand and began his defense.
text.DRB: But Agrippa said to Paul: Thou art permitted to speak for thyself. Then Paul stretching forth his hand, began to make his answer:

text.NRSV: “I consider myself fortunate that it is before you, King Agrippa, I am to make my defense today against all the accusations of the Jews,
text.Rotherham: Concerning all things of which I am accused by Jews, King Agrippa, I have been counting myself happy, that, before thee, am I about, this day, to be making my defence;
text.KJV: I think myself happy, king Agrippa, because I shall answer for myself this day before thee touching all the things whereof I am accused of the Jews:
text.NABRE: “I count myself fortunate, King Agrippa, that I am to defend myself before you today against all the charges made against me by the Jews,
text.DRB: Touching all things whereof I am accused of the Jews, king Agrippa, I account myself happy for that I am to defend myself this day before thee,

text.NRSV: because you are especially familiar with all the customs and controversies of the Jews; therefore I beg of you to listen to me patiently.
text.Rotherham: especially, as thou art, well-versed, in all the Jewish customs and questions. Wherefore, I beseech thee, patiently, to hear me.
text.KJV: Especially because I know thee to be expert in all customs and questions which are among the Jews: wherefore I beseech thee to hear me patiently.
text.NABRE: especially since you are an expert in all the Jewish customs and controversies. And therefore I beg you to listen patiently.
text.DRB: especially whereas thou knowest all things that are among the Jews, customs and questions: for the which cause I beseech thee, hear me patiently.

text.NRSV: “All the Jews know my way of life from my youth, a life spent from the beginning among my own people and in Jerusalem.
text.Rotherham: My manner of life, then, from my youth, which, from its commencement, was formed among my nation, even in Jerusalem, know all Jews,
text.KJV: My manner of life from my youth, which was at the first among mine own nation at Jerusalem, know all the Jews;
text.NABRE: My manner of living from my youth, a life spent from the beginning among my people and in Jerusalem, all [the] Jews know.
text.DRB: And my life truly from my youth, which was from the beginning in my nation in Jerusalem, all the Jews do know:

text.NRSV: They have known for a long time, if they are willing to testify, that I have belonged to the strictest sect of our religion and lived as a Pharisee.
text.Rotherham: inasmuch as they were aforetime observing me, from the outset,—if they please to bear witness,—that, according to the strictest sect of our own religion, I lived, a Pharisee.
text.KJV: Which knew me from the beginning, if they would testify, that after the most straitest sect of our religion I lived a Pharisee.
text.NABRE: They have known about me from the start, if they are willing to testify, that I have lived my life as a Pharisee, the strictest party of our religion.
text.DRB: knowing me before from the beginning (if they will give testimony) that according to the most sure sect of our religion I lived a Pharisee.

text.NRSV: And now I stand here on trial on account of my hope in the promise made by God to our ancestors,
text.Rotherham: And, now, for the hope of the promise, unto our fathers, being brought to pass by God, am I standing to be judged,—
text.KJV: And now I stand and am judged for the hope of the promise made of God unto our fathers:
text.NABRE: But now I am standing trial because of my hope in the promise made by God to our ancestors.
text.DRB: And now for the hope of the promise that was made of God to our fathers, do I stand subject to judgement:

text.NRSV: a promise that our twelve tribes hope to attain, as they earnestly worship day and night. It is for this hope, your Excellency, that I am accused by Jews!
text.Rotherham: unto which [hope], our twelve-tribed nation, with intensity, night and day, rendering divine service, is hoping to attain—concerning which hope, I am being accused by Jews, O King!
text.KJV: Unto which promise our twelve tribes, instantly serving God day and night, hope to come. For which hope’s sake, king Agrippa, I am accused of the Jews.
text.NABRE: Our twelve tribes hope to attain to that promise as they fervently worship God day and night; and on account of this hope I am accused by Jews, O king.
text.DRB: the which, our twelve tribes serving night and day, hope to come unto. Of the which hope, o king, I am accused of the Jews.

text.NRSV: Why is it thought incredible by any of you that God raises the dead?
text.Rotherham: What! incredible, is it judged with you, that, God the dead doth raise?
text.KJV: Why should it be thought a thing incredible with you, that God should raise the dead?
text.NABRE: Why is it thought unbelievable among you that God raises the dead?
text.DRB: What incredible thing is it judged with you, if God raise the dead?

text.NRSV: “Indeed, I myself was convinced that I ought to do many things against the name of Jesus of Nazareth.
text.Rotherham: I, therefore, imagined to myself, that, against the name of Jesus the Nazarene, it was needful, many hostile things, to bring about,—
text.KJV: I verily thought with myself, that I ought to do many things contrary to the name of Jesus of Nazareth.
text.NABRE: I myself once thought that I had to do many things against the name of Jesus the Nazorean,
text.DRB: And myself truly had thought that I ought to do against the name of Jesus of Nazareth many contrary things.

text.NRSV: And that is what I did in Jerusalem; with authority received from the chief priests, I not only locked up many of the saints in prison, but I also cast my vote against them when they were being condemned to death.
text.Rotherham: which also I did in Jerusalem, yea and, many of the saints, I myself, in prisons, shut up,—the authority, from the High-priests, having received: and, when they were to be put to death, I brought against them my vote;
text.KJV: Which thing I also did in Jerusalem: and many of the saints did I shut up in prison, having received authority from the chief priests; and when they were put to death, I gave my voice against them.
text.NABRE: and I did so in Jerusalem. I imprisoned many of the holy ones with the authorization I received from the chief priests, and when they were to be put to death I cast my vote against them.
text.DRB: Which also I did at Jerusalem, and many of the saints did I shut up in prisons, having received authority of the chief priests: and when they were put to death, I brought the sentence.

text.NRSV: By punishing them often in all the synagogues I tried to force them to blaspheme; and since I was so furiously enraged at them, I pursued them even to foreign cities.
text.Rotherham: and, throughout all the synagogues, ofttimes punishing them, I would fain have compelled them to defame; and, being excessively maddened against them, I went on to pursue them as far as even the outlying cities.
text.KJV: And I punished them oft in every synagogue, and compelled them to blaspheme; and being exceedingly mad against them, I persecuted them even unto strange cities.
text.NABRE: Many times, in synagogue after synagogue, I punished them in an attempt to force them to blaspheme; I was so enraged against them that I pursued them even to foreign cities.
text.DRB: And throughout all the synagogues oftentimes punishing them, I compelled them to blaspheme: and yet more mad against them, I persecuted them even unto foreign cities.

text.NRSV: “With this in mind, I was traveling to Damascus with the authority and commission of the chief priests,
text.Rotherham: Among which things, being on a journey unto Damascus, with the authority and commission of the High-priests,
text.KJV: Whereupon as I went to Damascus with authority and commission from the chief priests,
text.NABRE: “On one such occasion I was traveling to Damascus with the authorization and commission of the chief priests.
text.DRB: Among which things whiles I went to Damascus with authority and permission of the chief priests,

text.NRSV: when at midday along the road, your Excellency, I saw a light from heaven, brighter than the sun, shining around me and my companions.
text.Rotherham: at mid-day, on the road, I saw, O King, from heaven, above the splendour of the sun, shining around me, a light, and [around] them who, with me, were journeying;
text.KJV: At midday, O king, I saw in the way a light from heaven, above the brightness of the sun, shining round about me and them which journeyed with me.
text.NABRE: At midday, along the way, O king, I saw a light from the sky, brighter than the sun, shining around me and my traveling companions.
text.DRB: at mid-day, in the way, I saw (o king) from Heaven a light to have shined round about me and them that were in company with me, above the brightness of the sun.

text.NRSV: When we had all fallen to the ground, I heard a voice saying to me in the Hebrew language, ‘Saul, Saul, why are you persecuting me? It hurts you to kick against the goads.’
text.Rotherham: and, when we were all fallen to the ground, I heard a voice, saying unto me, in the Hebrew language—Saul! Saul! why, me, art thou persecuting? It is hard for thee, against goads, to be kicking!
