text.KJV: And when we were all fallen to the earth, I heard a voice speaking unto me, and saying in the Hebrew tongue, Saul, Saul, why persecutest thou me? it is hard for thee to kick against the pricks.
text.NABRE: We all fell to the ground and I heard a voice saying to me in Hebrew, ‘Saul, Saul, why are you persecuting me? It is hard for you to kick against the goad.’
text.DRB: And when all we were fallen down on the ground, I heard a voice speaking to me in the Hebrew tongue: Saul, Saul, why persecutest thou me? It is hard for thee to kick against the prick.

text.NRSV: I asked, ‘Who are you, Lord?’ The Lord answered, ‘I am Jesus whom you are persecuting.
text.Rotherham: And, I, said—Who art thou, Lord? And the Lord said—I, am Jesus, whom, thou, art persecuting!
text.KJV: And I said, Who art thou, Lord? And he said, I am Jesus whom thou persecutest.
text.NABRE: And I said, ‘Who are you, sir?’ And the Lord replied, ‘I am Jesus whom you are persecuting.
text.DRB: And I said: Who are thou Lord? And our Lord answered: I am Jesus whom thou doest persecute.

text.NRSV: But get up and stand on your feet; for I have appeared to you for this purpose, to appoint you to serve and testify to the things in which you have seen me and to those in which I will appear to you.
text.Rotherham: But rise and stand upon thy feet; for, to this end, have I appeared unto thee,—To appoint thee an attendant and a witness, both of the things as to which thou hast seen me, and of those as to which I will appear unto thee:
text.KJV: But rise, and stand upon thy feet: for I have appeared unto thee for this purpose, to make thee a minister and a witness both of these things which thou hast seen, and of those things in the which I will appear unto thee;
text.NABRE: Get up now, and stand on your feet. I have appeared to you for this purpose, to appoint you as a servant and witness of what you have seen [of me] and what you will be shown.
text.DRB: But rise up and stand upon thy feet: for to this end have I appeared to thee, that I may ordain thee a minister and witness of those things which thou hast seen, and of those things wherein I will appear to thee,

text.NRSV: I will rescue you from your people and from the Gentiles—to whom I am sending you
text.Rotherham: Rescuing thee from among the people, and from among the nations, unto whom, I, am sending thee—
text.KJV: Delivering thee from the people, and from the Gentiles, unto whom now I send thee,
text.NABRE: I shall deliver you from this people and from the Gentiles to whom I send you,
text.DRB: delivering thee out of the peoples and nations unto the which now I send thee,

text.NRSV: to open their eyes so that they may turn from darkness to light and from the power of Satan to God, so that they may receive forgiveness of sins and a place among those who are sanctified by faith in me.’
text.Rotherham: To open their eyes; that they turn from darkness unto light, and the authority of Satan unto God, that they may receive remission of sins, and an inheritance among them who have been made holy by the faith respecting me.
text.KJV: To open their eyes, and to turn them from darkness to light, and from the power of Satan unto God, that they may receive forgiveness of sins, and inheritance among them which are sanctified by faith that is in me.
text.NABRE: to open their eyes that they may turn from darkness to light and from the power of Satan to God, so that they may obtain forgiveness of sins and an inheritance among those who have been consecrated by faith in me.’
text.DRB: to open their eyes, that they may be converted from darkness to light, and from the power of Satan to God, that they may receive remission of sins, and lot among the saints by the faith that is in me.

text.NRSV: “After that, King Agrippa, I was not disobedient to the heavenly vision,
text.Rotherham: Wherefore, O King Agrippa,—I became not disobedient unto the heavenly vision;
text.KJV: Whereupon, O king Agrippa, I was not disobedient unto the heavenly vision:
text.NABRE: “And so, King Agrippa, I was not disobedient to the heavenly vision.
text.DRB: Whereupon, king Agrippa, I was not incredulous to the heavenly vision:

text.NRSV: but declared first to those in Damascus, then in Jerusalem and throughout the countryside of Judea, and also to the Gentiles, that they should repent and turn to God and do deeds consistent with repentance.
text.Rotherham: But—both to them in Damascus, first, and in Jerusalem, unto all the country of Judaea also, and unto the nations, I carried tidings—that they should repent, and turn unto God, and, works worthy of their repentance, should practise.
text.KJV: But shewed first unto them of Damascus, and at Jerusalem, and throughout all the coasts of Judea, and then to the Gentiles, that they should repent and turn to God, and do works meet for repentance.
text.NABRE: On the contrary, first to those in Damascus and in Jerusalem and throughout the whole country of Judea, and then to the Gentiles, I preached the need to repent and turn to God, and to do works giving evidence of repentance.
text.DRB: but to them first that are at Damascus, and at Jerusalem, and unto all the country of Jewry, and to the Gentiles did I preach that they should do penance, & turn to God, doing works worthy of penance.

text.NRSV: For this reason the Jews seized me in the temple and tried to kill me.
text.Rotherham: Because of these things, Jews seized me in the temple, and were attempting to slay me with their own hands.
text.KJV: For these causes the Jews caught me in the temple, and went about to kill me.
text.NABRE: That is why the Jews seized me [when I was] in the temple and tried to kill me.
text.DRB: For this cause the Jews, when I was in the temple, apprehending me, attempted meaning to kill me.

text.NRSV: To this day I have had help from God, and so I stand here, testifying to both small and great, saying nothing but what the prophets and Moses said would take place:
text.Rotherham: So then, having met with, the help that is from God, until this day, do I stand, witnessing to both small and great, nothing else saying, than those things which both the prophets, and Moses, did say should certainly come to pass:—
text.KJV: Having therefore obtained help of God, I continue unto this day, witnessing both to small and great, saying none other things than those which the prophets and Moses did say should come:
text.NABRE: But I have enjoyed God’s help to this very day, and so I stand here testifying to small and great alike, saying nothing different from what the prophets and Moses foretold,
text.DRB: But aided by the help of God, I stand until this day, testifying to small & to great, saying nothing beside those things which the Prophets did speak should come to pass, & Moyses;

text.NRSV: that the Messiah must suffer, and that, by being the first to rise from the dead, he would proclaim light both to our people and to the Gentiles.”
text.Rotherham: If, to suffer, the Christ was destined, if, the first of a resurrection of the dead, he is about to carry tidings, of light, both unto the people, and unto the nations.
text.KJV: That Christ should suffer, and that he should be the first that should rise from the dead, and should shew light unto the people, and to the Gentiles.
text.NABRE: that the Messiah must suffer and that, as the first to rise from the dead, he would proclaim light both to our people and to the Gentiles.”
text.DRB: if Christ were passible, if the first of the resurrection from the dead, he were to shew light to the people and to the Gentiles.

text.NRSV: While he was making this defense, Festus exclaimed, “You are out of your mind, Paul! Too much learning is driving you insane!”
text.Rotherham: Now, as he was saying these things in his defence, Festus, with a loud voice, saith—Thou art raving, Paul! Thy great learning, is turning thee round unto, raving madness.
text.KJV: And as he thus spake for himself, Festus said with a loud voice, Paul, thou art beside thyself; much learning doth make thee mad.
text.NABRE: Reactions to Paul’s Speech. While Paul was so speaking in his defense, Festus said in a loud voice, “You are mad, Paul; much learning is driving you mad.”
text.DRB: As he spake these things and made his answer, Festus with a loud voice said: Thou art mad, Paul; much learning turneth thee to madness.

text.NRSV: But Paul said, “I am not out of my mind, most excellent Festus, but I am speaking the sober truth.
text.Rotherham: But Paul—I am not raving (saith he), most noble Festus,—but, the declarations of truth and soberness, am I sounding forth:
text.KJV: But he said, I am not mad, most noble Festus; but speak forth the words of truth and soberness.
text.NABRE: But Paul replied, “I am not mad, most excellent Festus; I am speaking words of truth and reason.
text.DRB: And Paul said: I am not mad, most excellent Festus; but I speak words of verity & sobriety.

text.NRSV: Indeed the king knows about these things, and to him I speak freely; for I am certain that none of these things has escaped his notice, for this was not done in a corner.
text.Rotherham: For well-knoweth, the king, concerning these things, unto whom, with boldness of utterance, am I speaking; for, that these things are not hidden from him, I am well persuaded,—for, not in a corner, hath this thing been done.
text.KJV: For the king knoweth of these things, before whom also I speak freely: for I am persuaded that none of these things are hidden from him; for this thing was not done in a corner.
text.NABRE: The king knows about these matters and to him I speak boldly, for I cannot believe that [any] of this has escaped his notice; this was not done in a corner.
text.DRB: For the king knoweth of these things, to whom also I speak constantly. For I think none of these things to be unknown to him. For neither was any of these things done in a corner.

text.NRSV: King Agrippa, do you believe the prophets? I know that you believe.”
text.Rotherham: Believest thou, King Agrippa, in the prophets? I know that thou believest!
text.KJV: King Agrippa, believest thou the prophets? I know that thou believest.
text.NABRE: King Agrippa, do you believe the prophets? I know you believe.”
text.DRB: Believest thou the Prophets, king Agrippa? I know that thou believest.

text.NRSV: Agrippa said to Paul, “Are you so quickly persuading me to become a Christian?”
text.Rotherham: And, Agrippa, [said] unto Paul—Almost, art thou persuading, me, to become a, Christian!
text.KJV: Then Agrippa said unto Paul, Almost thou persuadest me to be a Christian.
text.NABRE: Then Agrippa said to Paul, “You will soon persuade me to play the Christian.”
text.DRB: And Agrippa said to Paul: A little thou persuadest me to become a Christian.

text.NRSV: Paul replied, “Whether quickly or not, I pray to God that not only you but also all who are listening to me today might become such as I am—except for these chains.”
text.Rotherham: And, Paul, [answered] —I could pray unto God that, both almost and altogether, not only thou but all they who are hearing me this day, might become such,—as even, I, am, excepting these bonds.
text.KJV: And Paul said, I would to God, that not only thou, but also all that hear me this day, were both almost, and altogether such as I am, except these bonds.
text.NABRE: Paul replied, “I would pray to God that sooner or later not only you but all who listen to me today might become as I am except for these chains.”
text.DRB: And Paul said: I wish of God, both in little, and in much, not only thee, but also all that hear this day, to become such as I am also, except these bands.

text.NRSV: Then the king got up, and with him the governor and Bernice and those who had been seated with them;
text.Rotherham: And the king rose up, and the governor, Bernice also, and they who had been sitting with them;
text.KJV: And when he had thus spoken, the king rose up, and the governor, and Bernice, and they that sat with them:
text.NABRE: Then the king rose, and with him the governor and Bernice and the others who sat with them.
text.DRB: And the king rose up, and the President, and Bernice, and they that sat by them.

text.NRSV: and as they were leaving, they said to one another, “This man is doing nothing to deserve death or imprisonment.”
text.Rotherham: And, retiring, they began conversing one with another, saying, Nothing worthy of death or of bonds, doth this man practise.
text.KJV: And when they were gone aside, they talked between themselves, saying, This man doeth nothing worthy of death or of bonds.
text.NABRE: And after they had withdrawn they said to one another, “This man is doing nothing [at all] that deserves death or imprisonment.”
text.DRB: And going aside, they spake among themselves, saying, That this man hath done nothing worthy of death or bands.

text.NRSV: Agrippa said to Festus, “This man could have been set free if he had not appealed to the emperor.”
text.Rotherham: And, Agrippa, unto Festus, said—This man might have been released, if he had not appealed unto Caesar.
text.KJV: Then said Agrippa unto Festus, This man might have been set at liberty, if he had not appealed unto Cesar.
text.NABRE: And Agrippa said to Festus, “This man could have been set free if he had not appealed to Caesar.”
text.DRB: And Agrippa said to Festus: This man might be released, if he had not appealed to Cæsar.

text.NRSV: When it was decided that we were to sail for Italy, they transferred Paul and some other prisoners to a centurion of the Augustan Cohort, named Julius.
text.Rotherham: Now, when it was determined that we should sail for Italy, they proceeded to deliver Paul, and certain other prisoners, unto a centurion by name Julius, of an Augustan band.
text.KJV: And when it was determined that we should sail into Italy, they delivered Paul and certain other prisoners unto one named Julius, a centurion of Augustus’ band.
text.NABRE: When it was decided that we should sail to Italy, they handed Paul and some other prisoners over to a centurion named Julius of the Cohort Augusta.
text.DRB: And after it was decreed that he should sail into Italy, and that Paul with other prisoners should be delivered to a Centurion named Julius, of the band Augusta,

text.NRSV: Embarking on a ship of Adramyttium that was about to set sail to the ports along the coast of Asia, we put to sea, accompanied by Aristarchus, a Macedonian from Thessalonica.
text.Rotherham: And, going on board a ship of Adramittium, about to sail unto the places along the coast of Asia, we put to sea, there being with us, Aristarchus, a Macedonian of Thessalonica;
text.KJV: And entering into a ship of Adramyttium, we launched, meaning to sail by the coasts of Asia; one Aristarchus, a Macedonian of Thessalonica, being with us.
text.NABRE: We went on board a ship from Adramyttium bound for ports in the province of Asia and set sail. Aristarchus, a Macedonian from Thessalonica, was with us.
text.DRB: we going up into a ship of Adrumetum, beginning to sail about the places of Asia, loosed from the land, Aristarchus the Macedonian of Thessalonica continuing with us.

text.NRSV: The next day we put in at Sidon; and Julius treated Paul kindly, and allowed him to go to his friends to be cared for.
text.Rotherham: and, on the next day, we put into Zidon,—and Julius, treating Paul, kindly, gave him leave to go unto his friends and refresh himself;
text.KJV: And the next day we touched at Sidon. And Julius courteously entreated Paul, and gave him liberty to go unto his friends to refresh himself.
text.NABRE: On the following day we put in at Sidon where Julius was kind enough to allow Paul to visit his friends who took care of him.
text.DRB: And the third day following we came to Sidon. And Julius entreating Paul courteously, permitted him to go to his friends, and to take care of himself.

text.NRSV: Putting out to sea from there, we sailed under the lee of Cyprus, because the winds were against us.
text.Rotherham: and, from thence putting out to sea, we sailed under the lee of Cyprus, because the winds were contrary;
text.KJV: And when we had launched from thence, we sailed under Cyprus, because the winds were contrary.
text.NABRE: From there we put out to sea and sailed around the sheltered side of Cyprus because of the headwinds,
text.DRB: And when we had loosed thence we sailed under Cypres: because the winds were contrary.

text.NRSV: After we had sailed across the sea that is off Cilicia and Pamphylia, we came to Myra in Lycia.
text.Rotherham: and, sailing across the sea which is off Cilicia and Pamphylia, we came down to Myra, a city of Lycia.
text.KJV: And when we had sailed over the sea of Cilicia and Pamphylia, we came to Myra, a city of Lycia.
text.NABRE: and crossing the open sea off the coast of Cilicia and Pamphylia we came to Myra in Lycia.
text.DRB: And sailing the sea of Cilicia and Pamphilia, we came to Lystra, which is in Lycia:

text.NRSV: There the centurion found an Alexandrian ship bound for Italy and put us on board.
text.Rotherham: And the centurion, there, finding a ship of Alexandria sailing for Italy, put us therein.
text.KJV: And there the centurion found a ship of Alexandria sailing into Italy; and he put us therein.
text.NABRE: Storm and Shipwreck. There the centurion found an Alexandrian ship that was sailing to Italy and put us on board.
text.DRB: and there the Centurion finding a ship of Alexandria sailing into Italy, removed us into it.

text.NRSV: We sailed slowly for a number of days and arrived with difficulty off Cnidus, and as the wind was against us, we sailed under the lee of Crete off Salmone.
text.Rotherham: And, for a good many days sailing slowly, and getting with difficulty over against Cnidus, the wind not suffering us to get on, we sailed under the lee of Crete, over against Salmone;
text.KJV: And when we had sailed slowly many days, and scarce were come over against Cnidus, the wind not suffering us, we sailed under Crete, over against Salmone;
text.NABRE: For many days we made little headway, arriving at Cnidus only with difficulty, and because the wind would not permit us to continue our course we sailed for the sheltered side of Crete off Salmone.
text.DRB: And whereas many days we sailed slowly, and were scarce come over against Gnidus, the wind hindering us, we sailed near Crete by Salmone:

text.NRSV: Sailing past it with difficulty, we came to a place called Fair Havens, near the city of Lasea.
text.Rotherham: and, with difficulty coasting it, we came to a certain place called Fair Havens, near to which was the city of Lasea.
text.KJV: And, hardly passing it, came unto a place which is called The fair havens; nigh whereunto was the city of Lasea.
text.NABRE: We sailed past it with difficulty and reached a place called Fair Havens, near which was the city of Lasea.
text.DRB: and with much ado sailing by it, we came into a certain place that is called Good-havens, nigh to the which was a city Thalassa.

text.NRSV: Since much time had been lost and sailing was now dangerous, because even the Fast had already gone by, Paul advised them,
text.Rotherham: And, when a considerable time had passed, and sailing was already dangerous, because, even the Fast, had already gone by, Paul began to advise,
text.KJV: Now when much time was spent, and when sailing was now dangerous, because the fast was now already past, Paul admonished them,
text.NABRE: Much time had now passed and sailing had become hazardous because the time of the fast had already gone by, so Paul warned them,
text.DRB: And when much time was spent, and whereas now it was not safe sailing, because the fast now was past, Paul comforted them,

text.NRSV: saying, “Sirs, I can see that the voyage will be with danger and much heavy loss, not only of the cargo and the ship, but also of our lives.”
text.Rotherham: saying to them—Sirs! I perceive that, with damage and great loss, not only of the cargo and of the ship, but even of our persons, shall the voyage certainly be attended.
text.KJV: And said unto them, Sirs, I perceive that this voyage will be with hurt and much damage, not only of the lading and ship, but also of our lives.
text.NABRE: “Men, I can see that this voyage will result in severe damage and heavy loss not only to the cargo and the ship, but also to our lives.”
text.DRB: saying to them: Ye men, I see that the sailing beginneth to be with hurt and much damage, not only of the lading and the ship, but also of our lives.

text.NRSV: But the centurion paid more attention to the pilot and to the owner of the ship than to what Paul said.
text.Rotherham: But, the centurion, by the master, and by the shipowner, was more persuaded than by the things which, by Paul, were spoken.
text.KJV: Nevertheless the centurion believed the master and the owner of the ship, more than those things which were spoken by Paul.
text.NABRE: The centurion, however, paid more attention to the pilot and to the owner of the ship than to what Paul said.
text.DRB: But the Centurion believed the governor and master of the ship, more than those things which were said of Paul.

text.NRSV: Since the harbor was not suitable for spending the winter, the majority was in favor of putting to sea from there, on the chance that somehow they could reach Phoenix, where they could spend the winter. It was a harbor of Crete, facing southwest and northwest.
text.Rotherham: And, the harbour being, incommodious, to winter in, the more part, advised to put to sea from thence, if by any means they might be able to reach Phoenix, to winter, [which was] a harbour of Crete, looking north-east and south-east.
text.KJV: And because the haven was not commodious to winter in, the more part advised to depart thence also, if by any means they might attain to Phenice, and there to winter; which is an haven of Crete, and lieth toward the south west and north west.
text.NABRE: Since the harbor was unfavorably situated for spending the winter, the majority planned to put out to sea from there in the hope of reaching Phoenix, a port in Crete facing west-northwest, there to spend the winter.
text.DRB: And whereas it was not a commodious haven to winter in, very many taking counsel appointed to sail thence, if by any means they might coming to Phoenice, winter there, a haven of Crete looking toward the Afrike and the Chore.

text.NRSV: When a moderate south wind began to blow, they thought they could achieve their purpose; so they weighed anchor and began to sail past Crete, close to the shore.
text.Rotherham: And, a south wind blowing softly, supposing they had secured their purpose, weighing anchor, they began to sail close in shore along Crete.
text.KJV: And when the south wind blew softly, supposing that they had obtained their purpose, loosing thence, they sailed close by Crete.
text.NABRE: A south wind blew gently, and thinking they had attained their objective, they weighed anchor and sailed along close to the coast of Crete.
text.DRB: And the south-wind blowing, they thinking that they had obtained their purpose, when they had parted from Asson, sailed along by Crete.

text.NRSV: But soon a violent wind, called the northeaster, rushed down from Crete.
text.Rotherham: But, after no long time, there beat down from it a tempestuous wind, called Euraquilo,—
text.KJV: But not long after there arose against it a tempestuous wind, called Euroclydon.
text.NABRE: Before long an offshore wind of hurricane force called a “Northeaster” struck.
text.DRB: But not long after, a tempestuous wind that is called Euro-aquilo, drove against it.

text.NRSV: Since the ship was caught and could not be turned head-on into the wind, we gave way to it and were driven.
text.Rotherham: and, the ship being caught and we not being able to bring her head to the wind, we let her go, and were borne along.
text.KJV: And when the ship was caught, and could not bear up into the wind, we let her drive.
text.NABRE: Since the ship was caught up in it and could not head into the wind we gave way and let ourselves be driven.
text.DRB: And when the ship was caught and could not make way against the wind, giving up the ship to the winds, we were driven,

text.NRSV: By running under the lee of a small island called Cauda we were scarcely able to get the ship’s boat under control.
text.Rotherham: And, running under the lee, of some small island, called Cauda, we were able, with difficulty, to make ourselves masters of the boat,—
text.KJV: And running under a certain island which is called Clauda, we had much work to come by the boat:
text.NABRE: We passed along the sheltered side of an island named Cauda and managed only with difficulty to get the dinghy under control.
text.DRB: And running upon a certain Island, that is called Cauda, we could scarce get the cock-boat.

text.NRSV: After hoisting it up they took measures to undergird the ship; then, fearing that they would run on the Syrtis, they lowered the sea anchor and so were driven.
text.Rotherham: which, hoisting up, they began to use, helps, under-girding the ship; and, fearing lest, on the sand-bank of Africa, they should run aground, lowering the gear, so, were they borne along.
text.KJV: Which when they had taken up, they used helps, undergirding the ship; and, fearing lest they should fall into the quicksands, strake sail, and so were driven.
text.NABRE: They hoisted it aboard, then used cables to undergird the ship. Because of their fear that they would run aground on the shoal of Syrtis, they lowered the drift anchor and were carried along in this way.
text.DRB: Which being taken up, they used helps, girding the ship, and fearing lest they should fall into the Syrt, letting down the vessel, so were they carried.

text.NRSV: We were being pounded by the storm so violently that on the next day they began to throw the cargo overboard,
text.Rotherham: But, we being exceedingly tempest-tossed, on the next day, they began to throw [cargo] overboard;
text.KJV: And we being exceedingly tossed with a tempest, the next day they lightened the ship;
text.NABRE: We were being pounded by the storm so violently that the next day they jettisoned some cargo,
text.DRB: And when we were mightily tossed with the tempest, the next day they cast forth.

text.NRSV: and on the third day with their own hands they threw the ship’s tackle overboard.
text.Rotherham: and, on the third day, with their own hands, the tackling of the ship, they cast away.
text.KJV: And the third day we cast out with our own hands the tackling of the ship.
text.NABRE: and on the third day with their own hands they threw even the ship’s tackle overboard.
text.DRB: And the third day with their own hands they threw forth the tacklings of the ship.

text.NRSV: When neither sun nor stars appeared for many days, and no small tempest raged, all hope of our being saved was at last abandoned.
text.Rotherham: And, neither, sun nor stars, appearing for many days, and, no small tempest, lying upon us, in the end, all hope that we should be saved, began to be taken from us.
text.KJV: And when neither sun nor stars in many days appeared, and no small tempest lay on us, all hope that we should be saved was then taken away.
text.NABRE: Neither the sun nor the stars were visible for many days, and no small storm raged. Finally, all hope of our surviving was taken away.
text.DRB: And neither sun, nor stars appearing for many days, and no small storm being toward, all hope was now taken away of our saving.

text.NRSV: Since they had been without food for a long time, Paul then stood up among them and said, “Men, you should have listened to me and not have set sail from Crete and thereby avoided this damage and loss.
text.Rotherham: But, when they had been, long without food, then, Paul, standing in the midst of them, said—Ye ought, indeed, Sir! yielding to me, not to have sailed away from Crete, to get this damage and loss. …
text.KJV: But after long abstinence Paul stood forth in the midst of them, and said, Sirs, ye should have hearkened unto me, and not have loosed from Crete, and to have gained this harm and loss.
text.NABRE: When many would no longer eat, Paul stood among them and said, “Men, you should have taken my advice and not have set sail from Crete and you would have avoided this disastrous loss.
text.DRB: And when there had been long fasting, then Paul standing in the midst of them, said: You should indeed, O ye men, have heard me, and not have parted from Crete, & have gained this hurt and loss.

text.NRSV: I urge you now to keep up your courage, for there will be no loss of life among you, but only of the ship.
text.Rotherham: And, now, I recommend you to be of good courage; for, loss of life, shall there be, none at all, from among you,—only the ship.
text.KJV: And now I exhort you to be of good cheer: for there shall be no loss of any man’s life among you, but of the ship.
text.NABRE: I urge you now to keep up your courage; not one of you will be lost, only the ship.
text.DRB: And now I exhort you to be of good cheer. For there shall be no loss of any soul among you, but of the ship.

text.NRSV: For last night there stood by me an angel of the God to whom I belong and whom I worship,
text.Rotherham: For there stood by me this night, belonging unto the God whose I am, unto whom also I am doing divine service, a messenger,
text.KJV: For there stood by me this night the angel of God, whose I am, and whom I serve,
text.NABRE: For last night an angel of the God to whom [I] belong and whom I serve stood by me
text.DRB: For an Angel of the God whose I am, and whom I serve, stood by me this night,

text.NRSV: and he said, ‘Do not be afraid, Paul; you must stand before the emperor; and indeed, God has granted safety to all those who are sailing with you.’
text.Rotherham: saying—Be not afraid, Paul! for, before Caesar, must thou needs stand. And lo! God hath granted to thee as a favour, all them who are sailing with thee.
text.KJV: Saying, Fear not, Paul; thou must be brought before Cesar: and, lo, God hath given thee all them that sail with thee.
text.NABRE: and said, ‘Do not be afraid, Paul. You are destined to stand before Caesar; and behold, for your sake, God has granted safety to all who are sailing with you.’
text.DRB: saying: Fear not Paul, thou must appear before Cæsar: and behold God hath given thee all that sail with thee.

text.NRSV: So keep up your courage, men, for I have faith in God that it will be exactly as I have been told.
text.Rotherham: Wherefore, be of good courage, Sirs; for I believe in God—that, so, it shall be, according as it hath been told me.
text.KJV: Wherefore, sirs, be of good cheer: for I believe God, that it shall be even as it was told me.
text.NABRE: Therefore, keep up your courage, men; I trust in God that it will turn out as I have been told.
text.DRB: For the which cause be of good cheer ye men: for I believe God, that it shall so be, as it hath been said to me.

text.NRSV: But we will have to run aground on some island.”
text.Rotherham: Upon a certain island, however, must we needs be wrecked.
text.KJV: Howbeit we must be cast upon a certain island.
text.NABRE: We are destined to run aground on some island.”
text.DRB: And we must come unto a certain island.

text.NRSV: When the fourteenth night had come, as we were drifting across the sea of Adria, about midnight the sailors suspected that they were nearing land.
text.Rotherham: And, when, the fourteenth night, had come, and we were being driven to and fro in the Adriatic, about midnight, the sailors suspected that some country was, nearing, them;
text.KJV: But when the fourteenth night was come, as we were driven up and down in Adria, about midnight the shipmen deemed that they drew near to some country;
text.NABRE: On the fourteenth night, as we were still being driven about on the Adriatic Sea, toward midnight the sailors began to suspect that they were nearing land.
text.DRB: But after the fourteenth night was come on us, as we were sailing in Adria about mid-night, the ship-men deemed that there appeared some country to them.

text.NRSV: So they took soundings and found twenty fathoms; a little farther on they took soundings again and found fifteen fathoms.
text.Rotherham: and, sounding, they found twenty fathoms,—and, going a little further, and again sounding, they found fifteen fathoms.
text.KJV: And sounded, and found it twenty fathoms: and when they had gone a little further, they sounded again, and found it fifteen fathoms.
text.NABRE: They took soundings and found twenty fathoms; a little farther on, they again took soundings and found fifteen fathoms.
text.DRB: Who also sounding, found twenty fathoms: and being parted a little from thence, they found fifteen fathoms.

text.NRSV: Fearing that we might run on the rocks, they let down four anchors from the stern and prayed for day to come.
text.Rotherham: And, fearing lest haply, on rocky places, we should be wrecked, out of stern, cast they four anchors,—and began praying that day might dawn.
text.KJV: Then fearing lest we should have fallen upon rocks, they cast four anchors out of the stern, and wished for the day.
text.NABRE: Fearing that we would run aground on a rocky coast, they dropped four anchors from the stern and prayed for day to come.
text.DRB: And fearing lest we should fall into rough places, casting out of the stern four anchors, they wished that day were come.

text.NRSV: But when the sailors tried to escape from the ship and had lowered the boat into the sea, on the pretext of putting out anchors from the bow,
text.Rotherham: But, when, the sailors, were seeking to flee out of the ship, and had lowered the boat into the sea, by pretext, as though out of the prow they had been about to reach anchors,
text.KJV: And as the shipmen were about to flee out of the ship, when they had let down the boat into the sea, under colour as though they would have cast anchors out of the foreship,
text.NABRE: The sailors then tried to abandon ship; they lowered the dinghy to the sea on the pretext of going to lay out anchors from the bow.
text.DRB: But as the ship-men sought to fly out of the ship, having let down the cock-boat into the sea, pretending as if they were about to cast out anchors out of the fore-part of the ship,

text.NRSV: Paul said to the centurion and the soldiers, “Unless these men stay in the ship, you cannot be saved.”
text.Rotherham: Paul said unto the centurion, and unto the soldiers—Except, these, abide in the ship, ye yourselves, cannot be saved!
text.KJV: Paul said to the centurion and to the soldiers, Except these abide in the ship, ye cannot be saved.
text.NABRE: But Paul said to the centurion and the soldiers, “Unless these men stay with the ship, you cannot be saved.”
text.DRB: Paul said to the Centurion and to the soldiers: unless these tarry in the ship you cannot be saved.

text.NRSV: Then the soldiers cut away the ropes of the boat and set it adrift.
text.Rotherham: Then, the soldiers cut away the ropes of the boat, and let her fall off.
text.KJV: Then the soldiers cut off the ropes of the boat, and let her fall off.
text.NABRE: So the soldiers cut the ropes of the dinghy and set it adrift.
text.DRB: Then the soldiers cut off the ropes of the cock-boat; and suffered it to fall away.

text.NRSV: Just before daybreak, Paul urged all of them to take some food, saying, “Today is the fourteenth day that you have been in suspense and remaining without food, having eaten nothing.
text.Rotherham: And, until day was about to dawn, Paul continued to beseech one and all to take some food, saying—This day is, the fourteenth day, that, suspense, fasting, ye are completing,—having helped yourselves, to nothing.
text.KJV: And while the day was coming on, Paul besought them all to take meat, saying, This day is the fourteenth day that ye have tarried and continued fasting, having taken nothing.
text.NABRE: Until the day began to dawn, Paul kept urging all to take some food. He said, “Today is the fourteenth day that you have been waiting, going hungry and eating nothing.
text.DRB: And when it began to be light, Paul desired all to take meat, saying: This day is the fourteenth day that you expect and remain fasting, taking nothing.

text.NRSV: Therefore I urge you to take some food, for it will help you survive; for none of you will lose a hair from your heads.”
text.Rotherham: Wherefore, I beseech you to take some food,—for, this, lays a foundation for your safety; for, of no one of you, shall a hair of the head perish.
text.KJV: Wherefore I pray you to take some meat: for this is for your health: for there shall not an hair fall from the head of any of you.
text.NABRE: I urge you, therefore, to take some food; it will help you survive. Not a hair of the head of anyone of you will be lost.”
text.DRB: For the which cause I desire you to take meat for your health sake: for there shall not an hair of the head perish of any of you.

text.NRSV: After he had said this, he took bread; and giving thanks to God in the presence of all, he broke it and began to eat.
text.Rotherham: And, having said these things, and taken a loaf, he gave thanks unto God before all, and, breaking it, began to eat.
text.KJV: And when he had thus spoken, he took bread, and gave thanks to God in presence of them all: and when he had broken it, he began to eat.
text.NABRE: When he said this, he took bread, gave thanks to God in front of them all, broke it, and began to eat.
text.DRB: And when he had said these things, taking bread he gave thanks to God in the sight of them all: and when he had broken it, he began to eat.

text.NRSV: Then all of them were encouraged and took food for themselves.
text.Rotherham: And, all becoming, of good cheer, they also, helped themselves to food.
text.KJV: Then were they all of good cheer, and they also took some meat.
text.NABRE: They were all encouraged, and took some food themselves.
text.DRB: And being all made of better cheer, they also took meat.

text.NRSV: (We were in all two hundred seventy-six persons in the ship.)
text.Rotherham: Now we were, in the ship, in all, about seventy-six souls.
text.KJV: And we were in all in the ship two hundred threescore and sixteen souls.
text.NABRE: In all, there were two hundred seventy-six of us on the ship.
text.DRB: And we were in all in the ship, souls two hundred seventy six.

text.NRSV: After they had satisfied their hunger, they lightened the ship by throwing the wheat into the sea.
text.Rotherham: And, when they were satisfied with food, they began lightening the ship, casting out the wheat into the sea.
text.KJV: And when they had eaten enough, they lightened the ship, and cast out the wheat into the sea.
text.NABRE: After they had eaten enough, they lightened the ship by throwing the wheat into the sea.
text.DRB: And being filled with meat, they lighted the ship, casting the wheat into the sea.

text.NRSV: In the morning they did not recognize the land, but they noticed a bay with a beach, on which they planned to run the ship ashore, if they could.
text.Rotherham: And, when day came, they could not recognise, the land; but perceived, a certain bay, having a beach,—upon which they were minded, if they could, safely to bring the ship.
text.KJV: And when it was day, they knew not the land: but they discovered a certain creek with a shore, into the which they were minded, if it were possible, to thrust in the ship.
text.NABRE: When day came they did not recognize the land, but made out a bay with a beach. They planned to run the ship ashore on it, if they could.
text.DRB: And when day was come, they knew not the land: but they spied a certain creek that had a shore, into the which they minded, if they could, to cast aland the ship.

text.NRSV: So they cast off the anchors and left them in the sea. At the same time they loosened the ropes that tied the steering-oars; then hoisting the foresail to the wind, they made for the beach.
text.Rotherham: And, casting off the anchors, they let them go into the sea,—at the same time, loosening the lashings of the rudders, and, hoisting up the foresail to the wind, they made for the beach.
text.KJV: And when they had taken up the anchors, they committed themselves unto the sea, and loosed the rudder bands, and hoised up the mainsail to the wind, and made toward shore.
text.NABRE: So they cast off the anchors and abandoned them to the sea, and at the same time they unfastened the lines of the rudders, and hoisting the foresail into the wind, they made for the beach.
text.DRB: And when they had taken up the anchors, they committed themselves to the sea, loosing withal the rudder bands: and hoisting up the main sail according as the wind blew, they went on toward the shore.

text.NRSV: But striking a reef, they ran the ship aground; the bow stuck and remained immovable, but the stern was being broken up by the force of the waves.
text.Rotherham: But, falling into a place where two seas met, they ran the ship aground; and, the foreship sticking fast, remained immoveable, while, the stern, began to break up, from the violence [of the waves].
text.KJV: And falling into a place where two seas met, they ran the ship aground; and the forepart stuck fast, and remained unmoveable, but the hinder part was broken with the violence of the waves.
text.NABRE: But they struck a sandbar and ran the ship aground. The bow was wedged in and could not be moved, but the stern began to break up under the pounding [of the waves].
text.DRB: And when we were fallen into a place between two seas, they graveled the ship: and the fore-part truly sticking fast remained unmoveable: but the hinder-part was broken by the violence of the sea.

text.NRSV: The soldiers’ plan was to kill the prisoners, so that none might swim away and escape;
text.Rotherham: Now, the soldiers counsel, turned out to be, that they should kill, the prisoners, lest any one should swim out and escape;
text.KJV: And the soldiers’ counsel was to kill the prisoners, lest any of them should swim out, and escape.
text.NABRE: The soldiers planned to kill the prisoners so that none might swim away and escape,
text.DRB: And the counsel of the soldiers was, that they should kill the prisoners: lest any swimming out, might run away.

text.NRSV: but the centurion, wishing to save Paul, kept them from carrying out their plan. He ordered those who could swim to jump overboard first and make for the land,
text.Rotherham: but, the centurion, being minded to bring Paul safely through, hindered them of their purpose, and ordered such as were able to swim, to cast themselves overboard and, get first to the land,—
text.KJV: But the centurion, willing to save Paul, kept them from their purpose; and commanded that they which could swim should cast themselves first into the sea, and get to land:
text.NABRE: but the centurion wanted to save Paul and so kept them from carrying out their plan. He ordered those who could swim to jump overboard first and get to the shore,
text.DRB: But the Centurion willing to save Paul, forbade it to be done: & he commanded them that could swim, to cast out themselves first, and escape, and go forth to land:

text.NRSV: and the rest to follow, some on planks and others on pieces of the ship. And so it was that all were brought safely to land.
text.Rotherham: and, the rest, some, on planks, and, some, on other things from the ship, …and, so, it came to pass, that, all, were brought safely through, on to the land.
text.KJV: And the rest, some on boards, and some on broken pieces of the ship. And so it came to pass, that they escaped all safe to land.
text.NABRE: and then the rest, some on planks, others on debris from the ship. In this way, all reached shore safely.
text.DRB: and the rest, some they carried on boards, & some upon those things that were of the ship. And so it came to pass, that all the souls escaped to land.

text.KJV: And when they were escaped, then they knew that the island was called Melita.
text.NABRE: Once we had reached safety we learned that the island was called Malta.
text.NRSV: After we had reached safety, we then learned that the island was called Malta.
text.DRB: And when we had escaped, then we knew that the island was called Mitilene. But the Barbarous shewed us no small courtesy.
text.Rotherham: And, when we were safely through, then, we knew that the island was called, Melita.

text.KJV: And the barbarous people shewed us no little kindness: for they kindled a fire, and received us every one, because of the present rain, and because of the cold.
text.NABRE: The natives showed us extraordinary hospitality; they lit a fire and welcomed all of us because it had begun to rain and was cold.
text.NRSV: The natives showed us unusual kindness. Since it had begun to rain and was cold, they kindled a fire and welcomed all of us around it.
text.DRB: For, kindling a fire they refreshed us all, because of the imminent rain and the cold.
text.Rotherham: And, the natives, began to shew us no common philanthropy; for, kindling a fire, they received us all, because of the rain that had set in, and because of the cold.

text.KJV: And when Paul had gathered a bundle of sticks, and laid them on the fire, there came a viper out of the heat, and fastened on his hand.
text.NABRE: Paul had gathered a bundle of brushwood and was putting it on the fire when a viper, escaping from the heat, fastened on his hand.
text.NRSV: Paul had gathered a bundle of brushwood and was putting it on the fire, when a viper, driven out by the heat, fastened itself on his hand.
text.DRB: And when Paul had gathered together some number of sticks, and had laid them on the fire, a viper issuing out of the heat, invaded his hand.
text.Rotherham: But, when Paul had gathered a certain lot of firewood into a bundle, and laid it on the fire, a viper, by reason of the heat, coming forth, fastened on his hand.

text.KJV: And when the barbarians saw the venomous beast hang on his hand, they said among themselves, No doubt this man is a murderer, whom, though he hath escaped the sea, yet vengeance suffereth not to live.
text.NABRE: When the natives saw the snake hanging from his hand, they said to one another, “This man must certainly be a murderer; though he escaped the sea, Justice has not let him remain alive.”
text.NRSV: When the natives saw the creature hanging from his hand, they said to one another, “This man must be a murderer; though he has escaped from the sea, justice has not allowed him to live.”
text.DRB: But as the Barbarous saw the beast hanging on his hand, they said one to another: Undoubtedly this man is a murderer, who being escaped out of the sea, vengeance doth not suffer him to live.
text.Rotherham: And, when the natives saw the brute hanging out from his hand, they began to say, one to another—Doubtless, this man is, a murderer, whom, though brought safely through out of the sea, Justice, hath not suffered, to live.

text.KJV: And he shook off the beast into the fire, and felt no harm.
text.NABRE: But he shook the snake off into the fire and suffered no harm.
text.NRSV: He, however, shook off the creature into the fire and suffered no harm.
text.DRB: and he indeed shaking off the beast into the fire, suffered no harm.
text.Rotherham: He, however, shaking of the brute into the fire, suffered no harm;

text.KJV: Howbeit they looked when he should have swollen, or fallen down dead suddenly: but after they had looked a great while, and saw no harm come to him, they changed their minds, and said that he was a god.
text.NABRE: They were expecting him to swell up or suddenly to fall down dead but, after waiting a long time and seeing nothing unusual happen to him, they changed their minds and began to say that he was a god.
text.NRSV: They were expecting him to swell up or drop dead, but after they had waited a long time and saw that nothing unusual had happened to him, they changed their minds and began to say that he was a god.
text.DRB: But they supposed that he should be turned into a swelling, and that he would suddenly fall and die. But expecting long and seeing that there was no harm done on him, being changed they said, that he was a God.
text.Rotherham: whereas, they, were expecting, that he was about to become inflamed, or to fall down suddenly dead;—but, when they had been long expecting, and had observed, nothing unusual, happening unto him, they changed their minds, and began to say he was a god.

text.KJV: In the same quarters were possessions of the chief man of the island, whose name was Publius; who received us, and lodged us three days courteously.
text.NABRE: In the vicinity of that place were lands belonging to a man named Publius, the chief of the island. He welcomed us and received us cordially as his guests for three days.
text.NRSV: Now in the neighborhood of that place were lands belonging to the leading man of the island, named Publius, who received us and entertained us hospitably for three days.
text.DRB: And in those places were lands of the prince of the isle, named Publius, who receiving us, for three days entreated us courteously.
text.Rotherham: Now, in the neighbourhood of that place, were lands, belonging to the chief man of the island, by name Publius,—who, making us welcome, for three days, hospitably entertained us.

text.KJV: And it came to pass, that the father of Publius lay sick of a fever and of a bloody flux: to whom Paul entered in, and prayed, and laid his hands on him, and healed him.
text.NABRE: It so happened that the father of Publius was sick with a fever and dysentery. Paul visited him and, after praying, laid his hands on him and healed him.
text.NRSV: It so happened that the father of Publius lay sick in bed with fever and dysentery. Paul visited him and cured him by praying and putting his hands on him.
text.DRB: And it chanced that the father of Publius lay vexed with fevers and the bloody flux. Unto whom Paul entered: and when he had prayed, and imposed hands on him, he healed him.
text.Rotherham: And it so happened that, the father of Publius, with feverish heats and dysentery distressed, was lying prostrate: unto whom Paul entering in and praying, laid his hands on him, and healed him.

text.KJV: So when this was done, others also, which had diseases in the island, came, and were healed:
text.NABRE: After this had taken place, the rest of the sick on the island came to Paul and were cured.
text.NRSV: After this happened, the rest of the people on the island who had diseases also came and were cured.
text.DRB: Which being done, all in the isle also that had infirmities, came, and were cured:
text.Rotherham: And, when this happened, the rest also, even they in the island who were sick, were coming in and getting cured;

text.KJV: Who also honoured us with many honours; and when we departed, they laded us with such things as were necessary.
text.NABRE: They paid us great honor and when we eventually set sail they brought us the provisions we needed.
text.NRSV: They bestowed many honors on us, and when we were about to sail, they put on board all the provisions we needed.
text.DRB: who also honoured us with many honours, and when we were sailing away, laded us with necessaries.
text.Rotherham: who also, with many honours, honoured us, and, when we were about to sail, put on board such things as we might need.

text.KJV: And after three months we departed in a ship of Alexandria, which had wintered in the isle, whose sign was Castor and Pollux.
text.NABRE: Arrival in Rome. Three months later we set sail on a ship that had wintered at the island. It was an Alexandrian ship with the Dioscuri as its figurehead.
text.NRSV: Three months later we set sail on a ship that had wintered at the island, an Alexandrian ship with the Twin Brothers as its figurehead.
text.DRB: And after three months, we sailed in a ship of Alexandria, that had wintered in the island, whose sign was the Castors.
text.Rotherham: And, after three months, we sailed in a ship which had wintered in the island, [a ship] of Alexandria, whose ensign was The Twin Brothers;

text.KJV: And landing at Syracuse, we tarried there three days.
text.NABRE: We put in at Syracuse and stayed there three days,
text.NRSV: We put in at Syracuse and stayed there for three days;
text.DRB: And when we were come to Syracusa, we tarried there three days.
text.Rotherham: and, touching at Syracuse, we tarried three days;

text.KJV: And from thence we fetched a compass, and came to Rhegium: and after one day the south wind blew, and we came the next day to Puteoli:
text.NABRE: and from there we sailed round the coast and arrived at Rhegium. After a day, a south wind came up and in two days we reached Puteoli.
text.NRSV: then we weighed anchor and came to Rhegium. After one day there a south wind sprang up, and on the second day we came to Puteoli.
text.DRB: Thence compassing by the shore, we came to Rhegium: and after one day the South-wind blowing, we came the second day to Puteoli,
text.Rotherham: whence, going round, we reached Rhegium; and, after one day, a south wind springing up, on the second day, we came to Puteoli;

text.KJV: Where we found brethren, and were desired to tarry with them seven days: and so we went toward Rome.
text.NABRE: There we found some brothers and were urged to stay with them for seven days. And thus we came to Rome.
text.NRSV: There we found believers and were invited to stay with them for seven days. And so we came to Rome.
text.DRB: where finding brethren, we were desired to tarry with them seven days: and so we came to Rome.
text.Rotherham: where, finding brethren, we were entreated to tarry, with them, seven days;—and, thus, towards Rome, we came.

text.KJV: And from thence, when the brethren heard of us, they came to meet us as far as Appii forum, and The three taverns: whom when Paul saw, he thanked God, and took courage.
text.NABRE: The brothers from there heard about us and came as far as the Forum of Appius and Three Taverns to meet us. On seeing them, Paul gave thanks to God and took courage.
text.NRSV: The believers from there, when they heard of us, came as far as the Forum of Appius and Three Taverns to meet us. On seeing them, Paul thanked God and took courage.
text.DRB: And from thence, when the Brethren had heard, they came to meet us unto Apij-forum, and the Three-taverns, whom when Paul had seen, giving thanks to God, he took courage.
text.Rotherham: And, when from thence the brethren heard the tidings concerning us, they came to meet us, as far as The Market of Appius and the Three Taverns—seeing whom, Paul thanked God, and took courage.

text.KJV: And when we came to Rome, the centurion delivered the prisoners to the captain of the guard: but Paul was suffered to dwell by himself with a soldier that kept him.
text.NABRE: When he entered Rome, Paul was allowed to live by himself, with the soldier who was guarding him.
text.NRSV: When we came into Rome, Paul was allowed to live by himself, with the soldier who was guarding him.
text.DRB: And when we were come to Rome, Paul was permitted to remain to himself with a soldier that kept him.
text.Rotherham: And, when we entered into Rome, Paul was suffered to abide by himself, with the soldier that guarded him.

text.KJV: And it came to pass, that after three days Paul called the chief of the Jews together: and when they were come together, he said unto them, Men and brethren, though I have committed nothing against the people, or customs of our fathers, yet was I delivered prisoner from Jerusalem into the hands of the Romans.
text.NABRE: Testimony to Jews in Rome. Three days later he called together the leaders of the Jews. When they had gathered he said to them, “My brothers, although I had done nothing against our people or our ancestral customs, I was handed over to the Romans as a prisoner from Jerusalem.
text.NRSV: Three days later he called together the local leaders of the Jews. When they had assembled, he said to them, “Brothers, though I had done nothing against our people or the customs of our ancestors, yet I was arrested in Jerusalem and handed over to the Romans.
text.DRB: And after the third day he called together the chief of the Jews. And when they were assembled, he said to them: Men brethren, I doing nothing against the people, or the custom of the fathers, was delivered prisoner from Jerusalem into the hands of the Romans,
text.Rotherham: And it came to pass, after three days, that he called together those who were the chief of the Jews; and, when they came together, he began to say unto them—I, brethren, though I had done nothing against the people, or the customs of our fathers, as a prisoner, out of Jerusalem, was delivered into the hands of the Romans;

text.KJV: Who, when they had examined me, would have let me go, because there was no cause of death in me.
text.NABRE: After trying my case the Romans wanted to release me, because they found nothing against me deserving the death penalty.
text.NRSV: When they had examined me, the Romans wanted to release me, because there was no reason for the death penalty in my case.
text.DRB: who when they had examined me, would have released me, for that there was no cause of death in me.
text.Rotherham: who, indeed, when they had examined me, were minded to set me at liberty, because there was, nothing worthy of death, in me.

text.KJV: But when the Jews spake against it, I was constrained to appeal unto Cesar; not that I had ought to accuse my nation of.
text.NABRE: But when the Jews objected, I was obliged to appeal to Caesar, even though I had no accusation to make against my own nation.
text.NRSV: But when the Jews objected, I was compelled to appeal to the emperor—even though I had no charge to bring against my nation.
text.DRB: But the Jews contradicting it, I was compelled to appeal unto Cæsar, not as having any thing to accuse my nation.
text.Rotherham: But, as the Jews spake against it, I was constrained to appeal unto Caesar,—not as though, against my nation, I had anything to bring by way of accusation.

text.KJV: For this cause therefore have I called for you, to see you, and to speak with you: because that for the hope of Israel I am bound with this chain.
text.NABRE: This is the reason, then, I have requested to see you and to speak with you, for it is on account of the hope of Israel that I wear these chains.”
text.NRSV: For this reason therefore I have asked to see you and speak with you, since it is for the sake of the hope of Israel that I am bound with this chain.”
text.DRB: For this cause therefore I desired to see you and to speak to you. For, because of the hope of Israel, am I compassed with this chain.
text.Rotherham: For this cause, therefore, have I called for you, to see and to speak with you; for, on account of the hope of Israel, this chain, have I about me!

text.KJV: And they said unto him, We neither received letters out of Judea concerning thee, neither any of the brethren that came shewed or spake any harm of thee.
text.NABRE: They answered him, “We have received no letters from Judea about you, nor has any of the brothers arrived with a damaging report or rumor about you.
text.NRSV: They replied, “We have received no letters from Judea about you, and none of the brothers coming here has reported or spoken anything evil about you.
text.DRB: But they said to him: We neither received letters concerning thee from Jewry, neither did any of the brethren that came hither, report or speak any evil of thee.
text.Rotherham: And, they, said unto him—We, have neither received letters concerning thee from the Jews, nor hath anyone of the brethren who hath arrived reported or spoken concerning thee, anything ill.

text.KJV: But we desire to hear of thee what thou thinkest: for as concerning this sect, we know that every where it is spoken against.
text.NABRE: But we should like to hear you present your views, for we know that this sect is denounced everywhere.”
text.NRSV: But we would like to hear from you what you think, for with regard to this sect we know that everywhere it is spoken against.”
text.DRB: But we desire of thee to hear what thou thinkest: for concerning this sect, it is known to us that it is gainsaid every-where.
text.Rotherham: But we deem it well, that, from thee, we should hear what are thine opinions; for, indeed, concerning this sect, it is, known to us, that, everywhere, is it spoken against.

text.KJV: And when they had appointed him a day, there came many to him into his lodging; to whom he expounded and testified the kingdom of God, persuading them concerning Jesus, both out of the law of Moses, and out of the prophets, from morning till evening.
text.NABRE: So they arranged a day with him and came to his lodgings in great numbers. From early morning until evening, he expounded his position to them, bearing witness to the kingdom of God and trying to convince them about Jesus from the law of Moses and the prophets.
text.NRSV: After they had set a day to meet with him, they came to him at his lodgings in great numbers. From morning until evening he explained the matter to them, testifying to the kingdom of God and trying to convince them about Jesus both from the law of Moses and from the prophets.
text.DRB: And when they had appointed him a day, they came to him unto his lodging very many: to whom he expounded, testifying the Kingdom of God, and using persuasion to them of Jesus, out of the Law of Moyses and the Prophets, from morning until evening.
text.Rotherham: And, having arranged with him a day, there came unto him, to the lodging, a larger number; unto whom he proceeded to expound, bearing full witness as to the kingdom of God, and persuading them concerning Jesus, both from the law of Moses, and from the prophets,—from morning till evening.

text.KJV: And some believed the things which were spoken, and some believed not.
text.NABRE: Some were convinced by what he had said, while others did not believe.
text.NRSV: Some were convinced by what he had said, while others refused to believe.
text.DRB: And certain believed those things that were said: but certain believed not.
text.Rotherham: And, some, indeed, were persuaded by the things that were spoken, whereas, others, disbelieved;

text.KJV: And when they agreed not among themselves, they departed, after that Paul had spoken one word, Well spake the Holy Ghost by Esaias the prophet unto our fathers,
text.NABRE: Without reaching any agreement among themselves they began to leave; then Paul made one final statement. “Well did the holy Spirit speak to your ancestors through the prophet Isaiah, saying:
text.NRSV: So they disagreed with each other; and as they were leaving, Paul made one further statement: “The Holy Spirit was right in saying to your ancestors through the prophet Isaiah,
text.DRB: And whereas they did not agree among themselves, they departed, Paul saying one word: That well did the Holy Ghost speak by Esaie the Prophet to our fathers,
text.Rotherham: and, not being, agreed, among themselves, they began to leave,—Paul having said one thing—Well, did, the Holy Spirit, speak through Isaiah the prophet, unto your fathers,

text.KJV: Saying, Go unto this people, and say, Hearing ye shall hear, and shall not understand; and seeing ye shall see, and not perceive:
text.NABRE: ‘Go to this people and say: You shall indeed hear but not understand. You shall indeed look but never see.
text.NRSV: ‘Go to this people and say, You will indeed listen, but never understand, and you will indeed look, but never perceive.
text.DRB: saying: Go to this people, and say to them: With the ear you shall hear, and shall not understand: and seeing you shall see and shall not perceive.
text.Rotherham: saying—Go thy way unto this people, and say—Ye shall, surely hear, and yet will in nowise understand. And, surely see, and yet will in nowise perceive;

text.KJV: For the heart of this people is waxed gross, and their ears are dull of hearing, and their eyes have they closed; lest they should see with their eyes, and hear with their ears, and understand with their heart, and should be converted, and I should heal them.
text.NABRE: Gross is the heart of this people; they will not hear with their ears; they have closed their eyes, so they may not see with their eyes and hear with their ears and understand with their heart and be converted, and I heal them.’
text.NRSV: For this people’s heart has grown dull, and their ears are hard of hearing, and they have shut their eyes; so that they might not look with their eyes, and listen with their ears, and understand with their heart and turn— and I would heal them.’
text.DRB: For the heart of this people is waxen gross, and with their ears have they heavily heard, and their eyes they have shut: lest perhaps they may see with their eyes, and hear with their ears, and understand with their heart, and be converted, and I heal them.
text.Rotherham: For the heart of this people, hath become dense, and, with their ears, heavily, have they heard, and, their eyes, have they closed,—lest once they should see with their eyes, and, with their ears, should hear, and, with their hearts, should understand, and return,—when I would certainly heal them.

text.KJV: Be it known therefore unto you, that the salvation of God is sent unto the Gentiles, and that they will hear it.
text.NABRE: Let it be known to you that this salvation of God has been sent to the Gentiles; they will listen.”
text.NRSV: Let it be known to you then that this salvation of God has been sent to the Gentiles; they will listen.”
text.DRB: Be it known therefore to you, that this Salvation of God is sent to the Gentiles, and they will hear.
text.Rotherham: Be it, known unto you, therefore, that, unto the nations, hath been sent forth this salvation of God: they, will also hear.

text.KJV: And when he had said these words, the Jews departed, and had great reasoning among themselves.
text.DRB: And when he had said these things, the Jews went out from him, having much questioning among themselves.

text.KJV: And Paul dwelt two whole years in his own hired house, and received all that came in unto him,
text.NABRE: He remained for two full years in his lodgings. He received all who came to him,
text.NRSV: He lived there two whole years at his own expense and welcomed all who came to him,
text.DRB: And he tarried full two years in his hired lodging: and he received all that came in to him,
text.Rotherham: And he abode two whole years in his own hired house, and made welcome all who were coming in unto him,—

text.KJV: Preaching the kingdom of God, and teaching those things which concern the Lord Jesus Christ, with all confidence, no man forbidding him.
text.NABRE: and with complete assurance and without hindrance he proclaimed the kingdom of God and taught about the Lord Jesus Christ.
text.NRSV: proclaiming the kingdom of God and teaching about the Lord Jesus Christ with all boldness and without hindrance.
text.DRB: preaching the Kingdom of God, and teaching the things that concern our Lord Jesus Christ with all confidence, without prohibition.
text.Rotherham: proclaiming the kingdom of God, and teaching the things concerning the Lord Jesus Christ, with all freedom of speech, without hindrance.

15.18: To our Lord was his own work known from the beginning of the world.

23.26: writing a letter containing this much: Claudius Lysias to the most excellent President Felix, greeting.
